15.4.2  Wrapping Up We now recall the construction, encoding and decoding time complexity forC ‚ąó
. The construc- tion time forC in is 2 O(n 2 ) , which substituting forn, is 2 O ¬≥ 1 őĶ 4 log 2 ¬° 1 őĶ ¬Ę  ŐĀ
. The construction time for C out , meanwhile, is only poly(N). Thus, the overall construction time is poly(N)+2 O ¬≥ 1 őĶ 4 log 2 ¬° 1 őĶ ¬Ę  ŐĀ
.
As we have seen in Section 15.1, the encoding time for this code isO(N 2 ), and the decoding time isN O(1) +N¬∑2 O(n) =poly(N)+N¬∑2 O ¬≥ 1 őĶ 2 log ¬° 1 őĶ ¬Ę  ŐĀ
. Further, we have shown that the decod- ing error probability is exponentially small: 2 ‚ąí‚Ą¶( ő≥N n ) =2 ‚ąí‚Ą¶ ¬≥ őĶ 5 log(1/őĶ) N  ŐĀ
. Thus, we have proved the following result: 5 Theorem 15.4.1.For every constant p and0<őĶ<1‚ąíH(p), there exists a linear code C ‚ąó of block lengthNand rate at least1‚ąíH(p)‚ąíőĶ, such that (a) C ‚ąó can be constructed in timepoly(N)+2 O(őĶ ‚ąí5 ) ; (b) C ‚ąó can be encoded in time O(N 2 ); and (c) There exists apoly(N)+N¬∑2 O(őĶ ‚ąí5 ) time decoding algorithm that has an error probability of at most2 ‚ąí‚Ą¶(őĶ 6 N) over the BSC p
.
4 Note thatr=1‚ąíH( p ő≥)=1+ p ő≥log p ő≥+(1‚ąí p ő≥)log(1‚ąí p ő≥). Noting that log(1‚ąí p ő≥)=‚ąí p ő≥‚ąíőė(ő≥), we can deduce thatr=1‚ąíO( p ő≥log(1/ő≥)).
5 Below we have used the fact that for any constanta>0,őĶ ‚ąía log O(1) (1/őĶ) isO(őĶ ‚ąía‚ąí1 ).
308 
Thus, we have answered in the affirmative Question6.3.1, which was the central open ques- tion from Shannon‚Äôs work. However, there is a still somewhat unsatisfactory aspect of the result above. In particular, the exponential dependence on 1/őĶin the decoding time complexity is not nice. This leads to the following question: Question 15.4.1.Can we bring the high dependence onőĶdown topoly ¬° 1 őĶ ¬Ę in the decoding time complexity?
15.5  Discussion and Bibliographic Notes Forney answered Question 6.3.1in the affirmative by using concatenated codes. (As was men- tioned earlier, this was Forney‚Äôs motivation for inventing code concatenation: the implication for the rate vs. distance question was studied by Zyablov lateron.) We now discuss Question 15.4.1. For the binary erasure channel, the decoding time com- plexity can be brought down toN¬∑poly( 1 őĶ ) using LDPC codes, specifically a class known as Tornado codes developed by Luby et al. [ 118]. The question for binary symmetric channels, however, is still open. Recently there have been some exciting progress on this front by the construction of the so-called Polar codes.
We conclude by noting an improvement to Theorem 15.4.1. We begin with a theorem due to Spielman: Theorem 15.5.1([ 158]).For every small enoughő≤>0, there exists an explicit C out of rate 1 1+ő≤ and block length N, which can correct‚Ą¶ őľ ő≤ 2 (log 1 ő≤ ) 2 ¬∂ errors, and has O(N)encoding and decoding.
Clearly, in terms of time complexity, this is superior to the previous option in Section 15.4.1.
Such codes are called ‚ÄúExpander codes.‚ÄĚ One can essentially do thesame calculations as in Section 15.4.1withő≥=őė ¬≥ őĶ 2 log 2 (1/őĶ)  ŐĀ
.
6 However, we obtain an encoding and decoding time of N¬∑2 poly( 1 őĶ )
. Thus, even though we obtain an improvement in the time complexitiesas compared to Theorem 15.4.1, this does not answer Question15.4.1.
6 This is because we need 1/(1+ő≤)=1‚ąíőĶ/2, which implies thatő≤=őė(őĶ).
309 
310 
Chapter 16 Information Theory Strikes Back: Polar Codes We begin by recalling Question 15.4.1, which we re-produce for the sake of completeness: Question 16.0.1.Can we get to withinőĶof capacity forBSC p (i.e. rate1‚ąíH(p)‚ąíőĶ) via codes with block length and decoding times that arepoly(1/őĶ)?
In this chapter we introducePolar codes, a class of codes developed from purely information- theoretic insights. We then show how these codes lead to a resolution of Question 16.0.1, namely how to get arbitrarily close to capacity on the binary symmetric channel with block length, and decoding complexity growing polynomially in the inverse of the gap tocapacity. This answers in the affirmative one of the most crucial questions in the Shannon setting.
This chapter is organized as follows. We define the precise question, after some simplifi- cation, in Section 16.1. In the same section, we discuss why the simplified question solvesa much more general problem. We then switch the problem from an error-correction question to alinear-compressionquestion in Section 16.2. This switch is very straightforward but very use- ful in providing insight into the working of Polar codes. In Section 16.3we introduce the idea of polarization, which provides the essential insight to Polar codes. (We remark that this section onwards is based on notions from Information Theory. The reader unfamiliar with the theory should first consult Appendix Eto get familiar with the basic concepts.) In Section16.4we then give a complete description of the Polar codes, and describe the encoding and decoding algo- rithms. In Section 16.5we then describe the analysis of these codes. We remark that theonly complex part of this chapter is this analysis and the construction and algorithms themselves are quite simple (and extremely elegant) modulo this analysis.
311 
16.1  Achieving Gap to Capacity The goal of this section is to present a simple question that formalizes what it means to achieve capacity with polynomial convergence, and to explain why this is the right question (and how answering this positively leads to much more powerful results by standard methods).
Recall that forp‚ąą[0,1/2) the BSC p is the channel that takes as input a sequence of bitsX= (X 1 ,...,X n ) and outputs the sequenceY=(Y 1 ,...,Y n ) where for eachi,X i =Y i with probability 1‚ąípandX i 6=Y i with probabilityp; and this happens independently for eachi‚ąą{1,...,n}. We use the notationZ=(Z 1 ,...,Z n )‚ąąBern(p) n to denote the error pattern, so thatY=X+Z.
Our target for this chapter is to prove the following theorem: Theorem 16.1.1.For every p‚ąą[0,1/2)there is a polynomial 1 n 0 (¬∑)such that for everyőĶ>0there exist k,n and an encoder E:F k 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 and decoder D:F n 2 ‚ÜíF k 2 satisfying the following conditions: Length and RateThe codes are short, and the rate is close to capacity, specifically,1/őĶ‚Č§n‚Č§ n 0 (1/őĶ)and k‚Č•(1‚ąíH(p)‚ąíőĶ)¬∑n.
Running timesThe encoder and decoder run in time O(nlogn)(where the O(¬∑)notation hides a universal constant independent of p andőĶ).
Failure ProbabilityThe probability of incorrect decoding is at mostőĶ. Specifically, for everym‚ąą F k 2 , Pr Z‚ąąBern(p) n [m6=D(E(m)+Z)]‚Č§őĶ.
Theorem 16.1.1is not the ultimate theorem we may want for dealing with the binarysym- metric channel. For starters, it does not guarantee codes of alllengths, but rather only of one fixed lengthnfor any fixed choice ofőĶ. Next, Theorem 16.1.1only guarantees a small probabil- ity of decoding failure, but not one that say goes to zero exponentially fast in the length of the code. The strength of the theorem is (1) its simplicity - it only takes one parameterőĶand delivers a good code with rateőĶclose to capacity and (2) Algorithmic efficiency: the running time of the encoder and decoder is a polynomial in 1/őĶ. It turns out both the weaknesses can be addressed by applying the idea of concatenation of codes (Chapter 10) while preserving the strength. We present the resulting theorem, leaving the proof of this theorem from Theorem 16.1.1as an ex- ercise. (See Exercise 16.1.) Theorem 16.1.2.There exists polynomially growing functions n 0 : [0,1]‚ÜíZ + and T:Z + ‚ÜíZ + such that for all p‚ąą[0,1],őĶ>0there existsőī>0, a function k:Z + ‚ÜíZ + and an ensemble of function E={E n } n and D={D n } n such that for all n‚ąąZ + with n‚Č•n 0 (1/őĶ)the following hold: 1. The codes areőĶ-close to capacity: Specifically, k=k(n)‚Č•(1‚ąíH(p)‚ąíőĶ)n, E n :F k 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 and D n :F n 2 ‚ÜíF k 2
.
1 I.e.n 0 (x)=x c for some constantc.
312 
2. The codes correct p-fraction of errors with all but exponentially small probability: Specifi- cally Pr Z‚ąľBern(p) n ,X‚ąľU(F k 2 ) [D n (E n (X)+Z)6=X]‚Č§exp(‚ąíőīn).
3. Encoding and Decoding are efficient: Specifically E n and D n run in time at most T(n/őĶ).
16.2  Reduction to Linear Compression In this section we change our problem from that of coding for error-correction to compress- ing a vector of independent Bernoulli random variables i.e., theerror-pattern. (Recall that we encountered compression in Exercise 6.10). We show that if the compression is linear and the decompression algorithm is efficient, then this turns into a linear code with efficient decod- ing (this is the converse of what we saw in Exercise 6.11). By virtue of being linear the code is also polynomial time encodable, given the generator matrix. We explain this simple connection below.
Forn‚Č•m, we say that a pair (H,D) whereH‚ąąF n√óm 2 , andD:F m 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 forms anŌĄ-errorlinear compressionscheme for Bern(p) n ifHhas rankmand Pr Z‚ąľBern(p) n [D(Z¬∑H)6=Z]‚Č§ŌĄ.
We refer to the ratio m n as the(compression) rateof the scheme (recall Exercise 6.10).
Proposition 16.2.1.Let(H,D)be aŌĄ-error linear compression scheme forBern(p) n withH‚ąą F n√óm 2
. Let k=n‚ąím and letG‚ąąF k√ón 2 andG ‚ąó :F n√ók 2 be full-rank matrices such thatG¬∑H=0and G¬∑G ‚ąó =I k (the k√ók identity matrix). Then the encoder E:F k 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 given by E(X)=X¬∑G and the decoder D ‚Ä≤ :F n 2 ‚ÜíF k 2 given by D ‚Ä≤ (Y)=(Y‚ąíD(Y¬∑H))¬∑G ‚ąó satisfy for everym‚ąąF k 2 : Pr Z‚ąąBern(p) n [m6=D ‚Ä≤ (E(m)+Z)]‚Č§ŌĄ.
Remark 16.2.2.1. Recall that straightforward linear algebra implies the existence of matrices GandG ‚ąó above (see Exercise 16.2).
2. Note that the complexity of encoding is simply the complexity of multiplying a vector by G. The complexity of decoding is bounded by the complexity of decompression plus the complexity of multiplying byG ‚ąó
. In particular, if all these operations can be carried out in O(nlogn)time then computing E and D ‚Ä≤ takes O(nlogn)time as well.
313 
3. Note that the above proves the converse of Exercise6.11. These two results show that (at least for linear codes), channel and source coding are equivalent.
Proof.SupposeD(Z¬∑H)=Z. Then we claim that ifZis the error pattern, then decoding is successful. To see this, note that D ‚Ä≤ (E(m)+Z)=(E(m)+Z‚ąíD((E(m)+Z)¬∑H))¬∑G ‚ąó (16.1) =(E(m)+Z‚ąíD(Z¬∑H))¬∑G ‚ąó (16.2) =(E(m)+Z‚ąíZ))¬∑G ‚ąó (16.3) =E(m)¬∑G ‚ąó =m.(16.4) In the above ( 16.1) follows by definition ofD ‚Ä≤ , (16.2) follows from the fact thatE(m)¬∑H=m¬∑G¬∑ H=0, ( 16.3) follows by assumption thatD(Z¬∑H)=Zand (16.4) follows sinceE(m)=m¬∑Gand G¬∑G ‚ąó =I. Thus, the probability of a decoding failure is at most the probability of decompression failure, which by definition is at mostŌĄ, as desired.
Thus, our updated quest from now on will be to Question 16.2.1.Design a linear compression scheme forBern(p) n of rate at most H(p)+őĶ.
See Exercise 16.4on how one can answer Question16.2.1with a non-linear compression scheme.
In what follows we will introduce the polarization phenomenon that will lead us to such a compression scheme.
16.3  The Polarization Phenomenon 16.3.1  Information Theory Review The only information theoretic notions that we need in this chapter are that of Entropy (see Definition E.1.2) and Conditional Entropy (DefinitionE.2.2). We use the notationsH(X) to de- note the entropy of a variableXandH(X|Y) to be the entropy ofXconditioned onY. The main properties of these notions we will use are the chain rule (see Theorem E.2.4): H(X,Y)=H(Y)+H(X|Y), and the fact that conditioning does not increase entropy (see Lemma E.2.6): H(X|Y)‚Č§H(X).
314 
We also use the basic fact that the uniform distribution maximizes entropy (see LemmaE.1.3) and hence, H(X)‚Č§log|‚Ą¶| if‚Ą¶denotes the support ofX. A final fact that will be useful to keep in mind as we develop the polar codes is that variables with low entropy are essentially determined, and variables with low conditional entropy are predictable. We formalize this (with very loose bounds) below.
Proposition 16.3.1.LetőĪ‚Č•0.
1. Let X be a random variable with H(X)‚Č§őĪ. Then there exists an x such thatPr X [X6=x]‚Č§őĪ.
2. Let(X,Y)be jointly distributed variables with H(X|Y)‚Č§őĪ. Then the function A(y)=argmax x {Pr[X=x|Y=y]} satisfies Pr (X,Y) [X6=A(Y)]‚Č§őĪ.
We defer the proof to Section 16.6.1.
16.3.2  Polarized matrices and decompression We now return to the task of designing a matrixH(and corresponding matricesGandG ‚ąó ) such that the mapZ7‚ÜíZ¬∑His a good compression scheme. While we are seeking anm√ónrectangular matrices with some extra properties, in this section we will convert the question of constructing Hinto a question about squaren√óninvertible matrices.
For an invertible matrixP‚ąąF n√ón 2 , we consider its effect onZ=(Z 1 ,...,Z n )‚ąľBern(p) n
. Let W=(W 1 ,...,W n ) be given byW=Z¬∑P. Now consider the challenge of predicting theW i ‚Äôs as they arrive online. Thus, when attempting to predictW i , we get to see W <i ,(W 1 ,...,W i‚ąí1 ), which we can use to predictW i
. For arbitrary matrices, e.g. the identity matrix, seeingW <i gives us no advantage on predictingW i
. A matrix will be considered polarized if, for an appropriately large fraction ofi‚Äôs,W i ishighly predictablefromW <i
.
To formalize the notion of highly predictable, we turn to information theory and simply requireH(W i |W <i )‚Č§ŌĄfor some very small parameterŌĄof our choice.
2 With this definition, how manyi‚Äôs should be very predictable? Let S=S ŌĄ = { i‚ąą[n]|H(W i |W <i )‚Č•ŌĄ } denote the set of unpredictable bits. An entropy calculation will tell us how small we can hope Sto be. SincePis invertible, we have (see Exercise 16.5): H(W)=H(Z)=n¬∑H(p).(16.5) 2 Eventually we will setŌĄ=o(1/n).
315 
However, by the chain rule we have H(W)= n X i=1 H(W i |W <i ) = X i‚ąąS H(W i |W <i )+ X i6‚ąąS H(W i |W <i ) ‚Č§ X i‚ąąS H(W i |W <i )+(n‚ąí|S|)ŌĄ(16.6) ‚Č§ X i‚ąąS H(W i |W <i )+nŌĄ ‚Č§ X i‚ąąS H(W i )+nŌĄ(16.7) ‚Č§|S|+nŌĄ.(16.8) In the above, ( 16.6) follows by using definition ofS, (16.7) follows from the fact that conditioning does not increase entropy and ( 16.8) follows from the fact that uniformity maximizes entropy and soH(W i )‚Č§1. We thus conclude that, sinceŌĄis small,|S|‚Č•H(p)¬∑n‚ąínŌĄ‚ČąH(p)¬∑n. Thus, the smallest that the setScan be isH(p)¬∑n. We will allow anőĶnadditive slack to get the following definition: Definition 16.3.2(Polarizing matrix, unpredictable columns).We say that an invertible matrix P‚ąąF n√ón 2 is(őĶ,ŌĄ)-polarizingforBern(p) n if forW=Z¬∑P(whereZ‚ąąBern(p) n ) and S=S ŌĄ = { i‚ąą[n]|H(W i |W <i )‚Č•ŌĄ } we have|S|‚Č§(H(p)+őĶ)n. We refer to the set S as the set ofunpredictablecolumns ofP(and {W i } i‚ąąS as theunpredictable bitsofW).
We next show how to get a compression scheme from a polarizing matrix(without necessar- ily having an efficient decompressor). The idea is simple: the compressor simply outputs the ‚Äúunpredictable‚ÄĚ bits ofW. LetW S =(W i ) i‚ąąS , the compression ofZis simply (Z¬∑P) S
. For the sake of completeness, we record this in Algorithm 16.3.1.
Algorithm 16.3.1POLARCOMPRESSOR(Z,S) INPUT: StringZ‚ąąF n 2 and subsetS‚äÜ[n] OUTPUT: Compressed stringW‚ąąF |S| 2 ‚ä≤Assumes a polarizing matrixP‚ąąF n√ón 2 1:RETURN ( Z¬∑P ) S Equivalently, if we letP S denote then√ó|S|matrix whose columns correspond to indices in S, then the compression ofZisZ¬∑P S
. Thus,P S will be the matrixHwe are seeking. Before turning to the decompression, let us also specifyGandG ‚ąó for the linear compression scheme corresponding to Algorithm 16.3.1. Indeed, Exercise16.3shows thatG= ¬° P ‚ąí1 ¬Ę  ŐĄ S (where  ŐĄ Sis the complement of setS) andG ‚ąó =P  ŐĄ S
. In particular, the complexity of multiplying an arbitrary 316 
vector byPandP ‚ąí1 dominate the cost of the matrix multiplications needed for the encoding and decoding.
We finally turn to the task of describing the decompressor corresponding to compression with a polarizing matrixPwith unpredictable columnsS. The method is a simple iterative one, based on the predictor from Proposition 16.3.1, and is presented in Algorithm16.3.2.
Algorithm 16.3.2Successive Cancellation Decompressor SCD(W,P,S) INPUT:S‚äÜ[n],W‚ąąF S 2 andP‚ąąF n√ón 2 OUTPUT: e Z‚ąąF n 2 such that ( e Z¬∑P) S =W PERFORMANCEPARAMETER: 3 Pr Z‚ąľBern(p) n [SCD((Z¬∑P) S ,P,S)6=Z]‚ä≤Smaller is better 1:FORi=1 tonDO 2:IFi‚ąąSTHEN 3: f W i ‚ÜźW i 4:ELSE 5: f W i ‚Üźargmax b‚ąąF 2 ¬© Pr ¬£ W i =b|W <i = e W <i ¬§¬™ 6:RETURN e Z‚Üź e W¬∑P ‚ąí1 Next we argue that Algorithm16.3.2has low failure probability: Lemma 16.3.3.If P is(őĶ,ŌĄ)-polarizing forBern(p) n with unpredictable columns S, then the suc- cessive cancellation decoder has failure probability at mostŌĄn, i.e., Pr Z‚ąąBern(p) n [Z6=SCD((Z¬∑P) S ,P,S)]‚Č§ŌĄn.
Thus, ifPis(őĶ,őĶ/n)-polarizing forBern(p) n then the failure probability of the successive cancel- lation decoder is at mostőĶ.
Proof.LetW=Z¬∑P. Note that by Step 3in Algorithm16.3.2, we have for everyi‚ąąS, f W i =W i
.
For anyi6‚ąąS, by Proposition 16.3.1applied withX=W i ,Y=W <i ,őĪ=ŌĄwe get that Pr Z [W i 6=A i ( e W <i )]‚Č§ŌĄ, whereA i (¬∑) is the corresponding function defined fori6‚ąąS(in Proposition 16.3.1). By a union bound, we get that Pr Z [‚ąÉis.t.W i 6=A i ( e W <i )]‚Č§ n X i=1 Pr Z [W i 6=A i ( e W <i )]‚Č§ŌĄn.
Note that by step 5in Algorithm16.3.2and the definition ofA i (¬∑), we have f W i =A i ( e W <i ). But ifW6= e Wthere must exists a leastisuch that f W i 6=W i
. Thus, we get Pr[W6= e W]‚Č§ŌĄnand so probability that SCD(W S ,P,S)6=Zis at mostŌĄn.
3 Note that the Successive Cancellation Decompressor, and Decompressors in general are not expected to work correctly on every input. Thus, the INPUT/OUTPUTrelations don‚Äôt fully capture the goals of the algorithm. In such cases in the rest of the chapter, we will include a PERFORMANCEPARAMETER, which we wish to minimize that attempts to capture the real goal of the algorithm.
317

To summarize, in this section we have learned that to prove Theorem16.1.1it suffices to answer the following question: Question 16.3.1.Given anyőĶ>0, does there exist an(őĶ,őĶ/n)-polarizing matrixP‚ąąF n√ón 2 where n is bounded by a polynomial in1/őĶ; whereSCD(potentially a re-implementation of Algorithm 16.3.2) as well as multiplication and inversion byPtakes O(nlogn)time.
Next we will describe the idea behind the construction, whichwill answer the above ques- tion.
16.3.3  A polarizing primitive Thus far in the chapter, we have essentially only been looking at the problem from different per- spectives, but have not yet suggested an idea on how to get the codes or compression schemes that we desire (i.e., to answer Question 16.3.1). In this section we will provide the essence of the idea, which is to start with a simple and basicpolarizationstep, and then iterate it appropriately many times to get a highly polarized matrix. Indeed, it is this section that will explain the term polarization (and why we use this term to describe the matrices we seek).
Recall that the ultimate goal of polarization is to start with many bitsZ 1 ,...,Z n that are in- dependent and slightly unpredictable (ifpis small), and to produce some linear transform that concentrates all the unpredictability into fewer bits. We will first try to achieve this with two bits. Therefore we haveZ 1 ,Z 2 such thatH(Z 1 )=H(Z 2 )=pand we wish to produce two bits W 1 ,W 2 such that at least one of these is less predictable than eitherZ i
. Since there are only 4 possible linear combinations of two bits (overF 2 ) and three of them are trivial (0,Z 1 , andZ 2 ) we are left with only one candidate function, namelyZ 1 +Z 2 , so we will setW 1 =Z 1 +Z 2
. For W 2 we are left with the trivial functions: 0 carries no information and so is ruled out. Without loss of generality, the only remaining choice isW 2 =Z 2
. Thus, we look at this transformation: P 2 : (Z 1 ,Z 2 )7‚Üí(W 1 ,W 2 )=(Z 1 +Z 2 ,Z 2 ). (See Figure 16.1.) 318 
This is an invertible linear transformation given by the matrix P 2 = őľ 1  0 1  1 ¬∂
.
This in turn implies (see Exercise 16.6): H(W 1 ,W 2 )=2H(p).(16.9) However, some examination shows thatH(W 1 )>H(Z 1 ),H(Z 2 ). In particular, the probability thatW 1 is 1 is 2p(1‚ąíp)‚ąą(p,1/2), and sinceH(¬∑) is monotonically increasing in this interval (see Exercise 16.7) it follows thatH(W 1 )>H(p)=H(Z 1 )=H(Z 2 ). Thus,W 1 is less predictable than either of the input bits, and thanks to the chain rule: H(W 2 |W 1 )=H(W 1 ,W 2 )‚ąíH(W 1 )=H(Z 1 ,Z 2 )‚ąíH(W 1 )=H(Z 1 )+H(Z 2 )‚ąíH(W 1 )<H(Z 1 ),H(Z 2 ).
In other words, multiplying byP 2 has separated the entropy of two equally entropic bits into a more entropic bit and a (conditionally) less entropic one. Of course this may be only slight polarization, and what we are hoping for is many bits that are almostcompletely determined by preceding ones.
To get more polarization, we apply this 2√ó2 operation repeatedly. Specifically, letP 2 (Z 1 ,Z 2 )= (Z 1 +Z 2 ,Z 2 ). Then we letP 4 (Z 1 ,Z 2 ,Z 3 ,Z 4 )=(W 1 ,W 2 ,W 3 ,W 4 )=(P 2 (U 1 ,U 3 ),P 2 (U 2 ,U 4 )) where (U 1 ,U 2 )=P 2 (Z 1 ,Z 3 ) and (U 3 ,U 4 )=P 2 (Z 2 ,Z 4 ). Thus, the bitW 1 =Z 1 +Z 2 +Z 3 +Z 4 has higher en- tropy than sayZ 1 or evenU 1 =Z 1 +Z 3
. On the other hand,W 4 =Z 4 conditioned on (W 1 ,W 2 ,W 3 ) can be shown to have much lower entropy thanZ 4 (unconditionally) or evenU 4 =Z 4 condi- tioned onU 2
.
The composition above can be extended easily tonbit inputs, whennis a power of two, to get a linear transformP n (See Figure 16.2). We will also give this transformation explicitly in the next section).
It is also clear that some bits will get highly entropic due to these repeated applications ofP 2
.
What is remarkable is that the polarization is nearly ‚Äúperfect‚ÄĚ - most bitsW i have conditional entropy (conditioned onW <i ) close to 1, or close to 0. (We will show this result later on.) This leads to the simple construction of the polarizing matrix we will describe in the next section. A striking benefit of this simple construction is that multiplying a vector by eitherPorP ‚ąí1 only takesO(nlogn) time. Further, a version of SCD (Algorithm 16.3.2) is also computable in time O(nlogn) and this leads to an overall compression and decompression algorithms running in timeO(nlogn), which we describe in the next section.
We note that one missing element in our description is the challengeof determining the exact set of indicesSthat includes all the high-conditional-entropy bits. We will simply skirt the issue and assume that this set is known for a given matrixPand given to the compres- sion/decompression algorithms. Note that this leads to a non-uniform solution to compression and decompression problem, as well as the task of achieving Shannoncapacity on the binary symmetric channel. We stress that this is not an inherent problem with the polarization ap- proach. An explicit algorithm to computeS(or a small superset ofS) is actually known and we discuss this in Section 16.7.

16.4  Polar codes, Encoder and Decoder We begin with the description of polar codes along with the statement of the main results in Sec- tion 16.4.1. We explicitly state the encoding algorithm and analyze its runtime in Section16.4.2.
We present the decoder as well as its proof of correctness in Section 16.4.3.
16.4.1  The Code and Polarization Claims We are now ready to describe the code.
Definition 16.4.1(Basic polarizing matrix).We define the n√ón polarization matrixP n recur- sively for n=2,4,8,..., by describing the linear map P n :Z7‚ÜíZ¬∑P n
. For n=2andZ‚ąąF 2 2 we define P 2 (Z)=(Z 1 +Z 2 ,Z 2 ).
4 For n=2 t andZ=(U,V)forU,V‚ąąF n/2 2 we define P n (Z)=(P n/2 (U+V),P n/2 (V)).
Exercise 16.8talks about an explicit description ofP n as well as some extra properties.
In Section 16.5we show that this matrix polarizes qtickly asn‚Üí‚ąě. The main theorem we will prove is the following: 4 We will be usingP n to denote then√ónmatrix andP n to denote the corresponding linear map that acts onZ.
320 
Theorem 16.4.2((Polynomially) Strong Polarization).Fix p‚ąą(0,1/2)and constant c. There ex- ists a polynomial function n 0 such that for everyőĶ>0, there exists n=2 t with1/őĶ‚Č§n‚Č§n 0 (1/őĶ) and a set E‚äÜ[n]with|E|‚Č§(őĶ/2)¬∑n such that for every i6‚ąąE, the conditional entropy H(W i |W <i )is either less than n ‚ąíc , or greater than1‚ąín ‚ąíc
. Furthermore, if we let S={i‚ąą[n]|H(W i |W <i )‚Č•n ‚ąíc } then|S|‚Č§(H(p)+őĶ)¬∑n and the matrixP n is(őĶ,1/n c )-polarizing forBern(p) n with unpredictable columns S.
This theorem allows us to specify how close to zero the conditional entropy of the polarized bits should be. Notably, this threshold can be the inverse of an arbitrarily-high degree polyno- mial inn. We will prove Theorem 16.4.2in Section16.5, which will be quite technical. But with the theorem in hand, it is quite simple to complete the description ofthe Basic Polar Code along with the associated encoding and decoding algorithms, and to analyze their performance.
Definition 16.4.3(Basic Polar (Compressing) Code).Given0<p<1/2andőĶ‚Č§1/4, let n and S‚äÜ[n]be as given by Theorem 16.4.2for c=2. Then the Basic Polar Code with parameters p and őĶmapsZ‚ąąF n 2 to P n (Z) S
.
Proposition 16.4.4(Rate of the Basic Polar Code).For every p‚ąą(0,1/2)andőĶ>0, the rate of the Basic Polar Code with parameters p andőĶis at most H(p)+őĶ.
5 16.4.2  Encoding The description of the mapP n (Z) is already explicitly algorithmic, modulo the computation of the setS. For the sake of completeness, we write the algorithm below in Algorithm 16.4.1, assumingSis given as input, and argue its runtime.
Algorithm 16.4.1BASICPOLARENCODER(Z;n,S) INPUT:npower of 2,Z‚ąąF n 2 andS‚äÜ[n] OUTPUT: CompressionW‚ąąF S 2 ofZgiven byW= ( P n (Z) ) S 1:RETURNW= ( P(n,Z) ) S 2:FUNCTIONP(n,Z) 3:IFn=1THEN 4:RETURNZ 5:ELSE 6:LetU=(Z 1 ,...,Z n/2 ) andV=(Z n/2+1 ,...,Z n ) 7:RETURN(P(n/2,U+V),P(n/2,V)) Proposition 16.4.5.The runtime of theBASICPOLARENCODERalgorithm is O(nlogn).
Proof.IfT(n) denotes the runtime of the algorithm on inputs of lengthn, thenT(¬∑) satisfies the recurrenceT(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) (since all operations other than the two recursive calls can be done inO(n) time), which implies the claimed runtime.
5 Recall that we are trying to solve the compression problem now.
321 
16.4.3  Decoding Note that a polynomial time algorithm to compute Pr ¬£ W i =b|W <i = e W <i ¬§ givenb‚ąąF 2 and e W <i ‚ąąF i‚ąí1 2 would lead to a polynomial time implementation of the SUCCESSIVECANCELLA- TIONDECOMPRESSOR(Algorithm 16.3.2). However, by exploiting the nice structure of the Basic Polarizing Matrix, we will get anO(nlogn) algorithm without much extra effort. The key insight into this faster decoder is that the decoder works even ifZ‚ąľBern(p 1 )√ó¬∑¬∑¬∑√óBern(p n ), i.e., even when the bits ofZare not identically distributed, as long as they are independent. This stronger feature allows for a simple recursive algorithm. Specifically we use thefacts that: 1. IfZ 1 ‚ąľBern(p 1 ) andZ 2 ‚ąľBern(p 2 ) are independent thenZ 1 +Z 2 is a Bernoulli random variable. Letb + (p 1 ,p 2 ) denote the bias (i.e., probability of being 1) ofZ 1 +Z 2 (and so Z 1 +Z 2 ‚ąľBern(b + (p 1 ,p 2 )) (see Exercise 16.9).
2. IfZ 1 ‚ąľBern(p 1 ) andZ 2 ‚ąľBern(p 2 ) are sampled conditioned onZ 1 +Z 2 =a(for some a‚ąąF 2 ) thenZ 2 is still a Bernoulli random variable. Letb | (p 1 ,p 2 ,a) denote the bias ofZ 2 conditioned onZ 1 +Z 2 =a. (Note thatb | (p 1 ,p 2 ,0) is not necessarily equal tob | (p 1 ,p 2 ,1).) See Exercise 16.10for more.
We now use the functionsb + andb | defined above to describe our decoding algorithm. We switch our notation slightly to make for a cleaner description. Rather than being given the vectorW S ‚ąąF |S| 2 , we assume that our decoder is given as input a vectorW‚ąą(F 2 ‚ą™{?}) n where W i =? if and only ifi6‚ąąS.
The main idea behind the decoding algorithm is that to completeWto a vector inF n 2 , we can first focus on computing W[1,...,n/2] def =(W 1 ,...,W n/2 ).
It will turn out that we can use the fact thatW[1,...,n/2]=P n/2 (Z ‚Ä≤ 1 ,...,Z ‚Ä≤ n/2 ) whereZ ‚Ä≤ i ‚ąąBern(b + (p,p)) are independent.
6 We use recursion to solve this problem, and by computing theZ ‚Ä≤ i ‚Äôs along the way, we can in turn computeW[n/2+1,...,n]=P n/2 (Z n/2+1 ,...,Z n ). Note that hereZ i is no longer drawn from Bern(p) but instead we haveZ i ‚ąľBern(b | (p,p,Z ‚Ä≤ i‚ąín/2 )). Nevertheless, the Z i ‚Äôs are still independent. This stronger condition allows us to solve the problem recursively, as detailed below in Algorithm 16.4.2.
We assume thatb + andb | can be computed in constant time, and with this assumption it is straightforward to see that the above algorithm also has a runtime ofO(nlogn), by using the same recursive analysis that we used in the proof of Proposition 16.4.5. The correctness is a bit more involved and we argue this in the next lemma.
Lemma 16.4.6.LetZ‚ąľBern(p 1 )√ó¬∑¬∑¬∑√óBern(p n ), andW=P n (Z). Further letW ‚Ä≤ =(W ‚Ä≤ 1 ,...,W ‚Ä≤ n ) be given by W ‚Ä≤ i =W i if i‚ąąS and W ‚Ä≤ i =?otherwise. Let( e Z, e W,ŌĀ)=RPD(W ‚Ä≤ ;n,(p 1 ,...,p n )). Then, if H(W i |W <i )‚Č§ŌĄfor every i6‚ąąS, we have the following: (1) For every i, we have thatPr Z [W i =1|W <i = e W <i ]=ŌĀ i
.
6 Note that this follows from definition ofP n
.
322 
Algorithm 16.4.2BASICPOLARDECODER: BPD(W;n,p) INPUT:n(power of 2),W‚ąą(F 2 ‚ą™{?}) n , and 0‚Č§p<1/2 OUTPUT: e Z‚ąąF n 2 such that for everyieitherW i =? or ( e Z¬∑P) i =W i PERFORMANCEPARAMETER: Pr Z‚ąľBern(p) n [Z6=BPD(W ‚Ä≤ ;n,p)] whereW ‚Ä≤ S = ( Z¬∑P ) S andW ‚Ä≤ i =? ifi6‚ąą S 1:( e Z, e W,ŌĀ)=RPD(W;n,(p,¬∑¬∑¬∑,p)) 2:RETURN e Z 3:FUNCTIONRECURSIVEPOLARDECODER: RPD((W;n,(p 1 ,...,p n ))) INPUT:W‚ąą(F 2 ‚ą™{?}) n andp 1 ,...,p n ‚ąą[0,1].
OUTPUT: e Z, e W‚ąąF n 2 with e W=P n ( e Z) and ¬° e W ¬Ę S =W S
.ŌĀ=(ŌĀ 1 ,...,ŌĀ n )‚ąą[0,1] n
.‚ä≤ e Zis the main output while e WandŌĀwill help us reason about correctness.
PERFORMANCEPARAMETER: Pr Z‚ąľBern(p 1 )√ó¬∑¬∑¬∑√óBern(p n ) [ e Z6=Z] where e Zis the first element of the triple output by RPD((Z¬∑P n ) S ;n,(p 1 ,...,p n )) 4:IFn=1 andW 1 ‚ąąF 2 THEN 5:RETURN(W 1 ,W 1 ,p 1 ) 6:ELSE IFn=1 andW 1 =?THEN 7:RETURN(1,1,p 1 ) ifp 1 ‚Č•1/2 and (0,0,p 1 ) otherwise 8:ELSE‚ä≤n‚Č•2 9:W=(W (1) ,W (2) ) whereW (1) =W[1,...,n/2] andW (2) =W[n/2+1,...,n] 10:FORi=1 ton/2DO 11:letq i =b + (p i ,p n/2+i ) 12:Let ( e X, e W 1 ,ŌĀ 1 )=RPD(W (1) ;n/2,(q 1 ,...,q n/2 )) 13:FORi=1 ton/2DO 14:letr i =b | (p i ,p n/2+i , e X i ) 15:Let ( e Y, e W 2 ,ŌĀ 2 )=RPD(W (2) ;n/2,(r 1 ,...,r n/2 )) 16:Let e Z=( e X+ e Y, e Y), and let e W=( e W 1 , e W 2 ) andŌĀ=(ŌĀ 1 ,ŌĀ 2 ) 17:RETURN( e Z, e W,ŌĀ) 323 
(2)Pr Z ¬£ W6= e W ¬§ ‚Č§ŌĄn.
(3)Pr Z ¬£ Z6= e Z ¬§ ‚Č§ŌĄn.
Note that part (3) of Lemma 16.4.6proves the same decoding error bound that we proved in Lemma 16.3.3for the SUCCESSIVECANCELLATIONDECOMPRESSOR(Algorithm16.3.2). The correctness of BASICPOLARDECODER(Algorithm 16.4.2) follows immediately and we state this explicitly below (see Exercise 16.11).
Corollary 16.4.7.For every input n=2 t , p‚ąą[0,1/2), andW‚ąą(F 2 ‚ą™{?}) n , theBASICPOLAR DECODER(Algorithm 16.4.2) runs in time O(nlogn)and computes an output e Z‚ąąF n 2 such that P n ( e Z) i =W i holds for every i for whichW i 6=?. Furthermore if (1)Z‚ąľBern(p) n , (2)W S =P n (Z) S and (3) H(W i |W <i )‚Č§ŌĄfor every i6‚ąąS then Pr Z [ e Z6=Z]‚Č§ŌĄn.
Proof of Lemma 16.4.6.The main part of the lemma is part (1). Part (2) follows almost immedi- ately from part (1) and Proposition 16.3.1. And part (3) is straightforward. We prove the parts in turn below.
We begin by first arguing that for everynthat is a power of two: e W=P n ( e Z).(16.10) For the base case ofn=1, lines 5and7show thatW 1 =Z 1 as desired.
7 By induction (and lines12 and15) we have that e W 1 =P n/2 ( e X) and e W 2 =P n/2 ( e Y). Finally, line16implies that: P n ( e Z)=P n (( e X+ e Y, e Y))= ¬° P n/2 ( e X+ e Y+ e Y),P n/2 (Y) ¬Ę =( e W 1 , e W 2 )= e W, as desired. In the above, the second equality follows from the definition ofP n
.
Part (1) follows by induction onn. Ifn=1 (where say we call RPD((W 1 ),1,(p ‚Ä≤‚Ä≤ 1 ))), then the claim follows since here we haveŌĀ 1 =p ‚Ä≤‚Ä≤ 1 ,W 1 =Z 1 andZ 1 ‚ąľBern(p ‚Ä≤‚Ä≤ 1 ). For larger values ofn, we consider two cases (below we haveZ=(U,V)).
Ifi‚Č§n/2, then we haveW i =P n/2 (U+V) i (via definition ofP n ). Furthermore, (U+V)‚ąľ Bern(q 1 )√ó¬∑¬∑¬∑√óBern(q n/2 ). Thus, by the inductive claim, the recursive call RPD ¬° W ‚Ä≤ [1,...,n/2];n/2,(q 1 ,...,q n/2 ) ¬Ę satisfies ŌĀ i =ŌĀ (1) i =Pr (U+V)‚ąľBern(q 1 )√ó¬∑¬∑¬∑√óBern(q n/2 ) ¬£ W i =1|W <i = e W <i ¬§ =Pr Z‚ąľBern(p 1 )√ó¬∑¬∑¬∑√óBern(p n ) ¬£ W i =1|W <i = e W <i ¬§ 7 Note that when definingP n , the base case wasn=2 but note that if we started withn=1 and defineP 1 (Z)=W, then the resulting definition is the same as on the we saw in Definition 16.4.1.

.
Now ifi>n/2, note that the conditionW[1,...,n/2]= e W[1,...,n/2] is equivalent to the con- dition thatU+V= e XsinceW[1,...,n/2]=P n/2 (U+V) and e X=P ‚ąí1 n/2 ( e W[1,...,n/2]), where the latter equality follows from ( 16.10) and the fact that the mapP n/2 is invertible. .

Part (2) follows from Proposition 16.3.1. We first note that ifi‚ąąS, then by line5of the algo- rithm we haveW i = f W i
. Now assumei6‚ąąS. We claim that f W i =1 if and only ifŌĀ i =Pr Z [W i = 1|W <i = e W <i ]‚Č•1/2. To summarize the claims of this section, Theorem16.4.2guarantees the existence of a po- larizing matrix as desired to satisfy the information-theoretic conditions of Question 16.3.1.
And Proposition 16.4.5and Corollary16.4.7ensure that the encoding and descoding times are O(nlogn). This allows us to complete the proof of Theorem 16.1.1(modulo the proof of Theo- rem 16.4.2‚ÄĒ which will be proved in the next section).
Proof of Theorem16.1.1.Recall from Proposition16.2.1and Question16.3.1that it suffices to find, givenp‚ąą[0,1/2) andőĶ>0, an (őĶ,őĶ/n)-polarizing matrixP‚ąąF n√ón 2 withnbounded by a polynomial in 1/őĶ; such that multiplication byPand decompression takeO(nlogn) time.
Theorem16.4.2applied with parametersp,őĶandc=2 yieldsnand the matrixP=P n ‚ąąF n√ón 2 that is (őĶ,1/n 2 )-polarizing. Moreover, since Theorem 16.4.2also guaranteesőĶ‚Č•1/n, we have thatP n is (őĶ,őĶ/n)-polarizing. Furthermore, there exists a setS‚äÜ[n] with|S|‚Č§(H(p)+őĶ)nsuch 325 
thatH(W  ŐĄ S |W S )‚Č§őĶwhenW=Z¬∑PandZ‚ąľBern(p) n
. Given such a setSwe have, by Proposi- tion 16.4.5, that the time to compressZ‚ąąF n 2 to (Z¬∑P n ) S isO(nlogn). Finally Corollary 16.4.7 asserts that a decompression e Zcan be computed given (Z¬∑P n ) S in timeO(nlogn) and e Zequals Zwith probability at least 1‚ąíőĶthereby completing the proof of Theorem 16.1.1.
16.5  Analysis: Speed of Polarization We now turn to the most crucial aspect of polarization - the fact that it happens, and it is fast enough to deliver polynomial convergence to capacity. In this section we first give an overview of how we will think about polarization. We will then analyze the convergence by first explor- ing what happens in a single polarization step (i.e., the action ofP 2 ) and then showing how the local effects aggregate after log 2 nsteps of polarization. This will lead us to the proof of Theo- rem 16.4.2.
16.5.1  Overview of Analysis We start by setting up some more notation. Recalln=2 t
. In this section it will be convenient for us to give names to intermediate variables {Z (j) i } (i‚ąą[n],0‚Č§j‚Č§t) that are computed during the computation ofP n (Z 1 ,...,Z n ). LetZ (0) i =Z i
. For 1‚Č§j‚Č§t, let (Z (j) i ,Z (j) i+2 t‚ąíj )=P 2 (Z (j‚ąí1) i ,Z (j‚ąí1) i+2 t‚ąíj )=(Z (j‚ąí1) i +Z (j‚ąí1) i+2 t‚ąíj ,Z (j‚ąí1) i+2 t‚ąíj ).(16.11) for everyi‚ąą[n] such thatiandi+2 t‚ąíj are in the same ‚Äúblock at the jth‚ÄĚ level. i.e.,‚Ćąi/2 t‚ąíj+1 ‚ĆČ= ‚Ćą(i+2 t‚ąíj )/2 t‚ąíj+1 ‚ĆČ. (Alternatively one could sayiandi+2 t‚ąíj as in the samedyadic intervalof size 2 t‚ąíj+1
.) Further, Definition 16.5.1.We will say that a pair(i,i ‚Ä≤ )are jth-level siblingsif they are in the same block at the jth level and i ‚Ä≤ =i+2 t‚ąíj
.
Note that if one unravels the recursion in ( 16.11), then ifZ i ,Z i ‚Ä≤ are on the LHS, then (i,i ‚Ä≤ ) are siblings at thejth level.
We now claim that (see Exercise 16.12): P n (Z)=(Z (t) 1 ,...,Z (t) n ).(16.12) Figure 16.3illustrates the block structure ofP n and the notion ofjth level blocks and siblings at thejth level.
In what follows we will pick a randomi‚ąą[n] and analyze the conditional entropy ofZ (j) i |Z (j) <i asjprogresses from 0 tot(we independently pickifor different values ofj). Indeed, let X j =H(Z (j) i |Z (j) <i ).
ClearlyX 0 =H(p) sinceZ (0) i =Z i is independent ofZ <i and is distributed according to Bern(p).
In what follows we will show that for every constantciftis a sufficient large then with high 326

...
   ‚úā‚úĄ   ‚úā  Figure 16.3: Block structure of the Basic Polarizing Transform. Circled are a block at the 2nd level and two 2nd level siblings.
probabilityover the choice of i,X t 6‚ąą(n ‚ąíc ,1‚ąín ‚ąíc ). To track the evolution ofX j asjincreases, we will first try to analyzelocal polarizationwhich will study howX j compares withX j‚ąí1
. Def- inition16.5.2below captures the desired effect of a local step, and the following lemma asserts that the operatorP 2 does indeed satisfy the conditions of local polarization.
Definition 16.5.2(Local Polarization).A sequence of random variables X 0 ,...,X j ,...,with X j ‚ąą [0,1]islocally polarizingif the following conditions hold: (1)(Unbiased):For every j, and a‚ąą[0,1]we haveE[X j+1 |X j =a]=a.
(2)(Variance in the middle):For everyŌĄ>0, there existsőł=őł(ŌĄ)>0such that for all j, we have: If X j ‚ąą(ŌĄ,1‚ąíŌĄ)then|X j+1 ‚ąíX j |‚Č•őł.
(3)(Suction at the ends):For every c<‚ąě, there existsŌĄ=ŌĄ(c)>0such that (i) if X j ‚Č§ŌĄthen Pr[X j+1 ‚Č§X j /c]‚Č•1/2; and similarly (ii) if1‚ąíX j ‚Č§ŌĄthenPr[(1‚ąíX j+1 ‚Č§(1‚ąíX j )/c]‚Č•1/2.
We further say a sequence issimpleif for every sequence a 0 ,...,a j , conditioned on X 0 =a 0 ,...,X j = a j , there are two values a + and a | such that X j+1 takes value a + with probability1/2and a | with probability1/2.
Lemma 16.5.3(Local Polarization).The sequence X 0 ,...,X j ,...,with X j =H(Z (j) i |Z (j) <i )where i is drawn uniformly from[n]is a simple and locally polarizing sequence.
We will prove Lemma 16.5.3in Section16.5.2but use it below. But first let us see what it does and fails to do. While local polarization prevents the conditionalentropies from staying static, it doesn‚Äôt assert that eventually all conditional entropies willbe close to 0 or close to 1, the kind of strong polarization that we desire. The following definition captures our desire from a strong polarizing process, and the lemma afterwards asserts that local polarization does imply strong polarization.
Definition16.5.4((Polynomially) Strong Polarization).A sequence of random variables X 0 ,X 1 ,...,X t ,...
with X j ‚ąą[0,1] strongly polarizesif for allő≥>0there existőĪ<1andő≤<‚ąěsuch that for all t we have Pr[X t ‚ąą(ő≥ t ,1‚ąíő≥ t )]‚Č§ő≤¬∑őĪ t
.
327 
Lemma 16.5.5(Local vs. Global Polarization).If a sequence X 0 ,...,X t ,...,with X t ‚ąą[0,1]is sim- ple and locally polarizing, then it is also strongly polarizing.
Armed with Lemmas 16.5.3and16.5.5, proving Theorem16.4.2is just a matter of setting parameters.
Proof of Theorem 16.4.2.We assume without loss of generality thatc‚Č•1. (Proving the theorem for largercimplies it also for smaller values ofc.) Givenpandc‚Č•1, lető≥=2 ‚ąíc
. Lető≤<‚ąě andőĪ<1 be the constants given by the definition of strong polarization (Definition 16.5.4) for this choice ofő≥. We prove the theorem forn 0 (x)=max{8x,2(2ő≤x) ‚Ćąlog(1/őĪ)‚ĆČ }. Note thatn 0 is a polynomial inx. GivenőĶ>0, lett=max n ¬• log(8/őĶ) ¬¶ , l log(2ő≤/őĶ) log(1/őĪ) mo so that n=2 t ‚Č§max n 8/őĶ,2¬∑(2ő≤/őĶ) 1/log(1/őĪ) o =n 0 (1/őĶ).
Note that the choice oftgiveső≤¬∑őĪ t ‚Č§őĶ/2 andő≥ t =2 ‚ąíct =n ‚ąíc
. We also haven‚Č•4/őĶand thus 2n ‚ąíc ‚Č§2n ‚ąí1 ‚Č§őĶ/2. We show that for this choice andtandn, the polarizing transformP n has the desired properties ‚ÄĒ namely that the setSof variables of noticeably large conditional entropy is of small size.
We first show that the set of variables with intermediate conditional entropies is small. Let us recall some notations from above, specifically ( 16.11). Let (Z (j) i ) denote the intermediate results of the computationW=P n (Z)=(Z (t) 1 ,...,Z (t) n ), and letX j =H(Z (j) i |Z (j) <i ) for a uniformly random choice ofi‚ąą[n]. By Lemmas 16.5.3and16.5.5we have that the sequenceX 0 ,...,X t ,...
is strongly polarizing. By the definition of strong polarization,we have that Pr i‚ąą[n] ¬£ H(W i |W <i )‚ąą(n ‚ąíc ,1‚ąín ‚ąíc ) ¬§ =Pr i h H(Z (t) i |Z (t) <i )‚ąą(ő≥ t ,1‚ąíő≥ t ) i =Pr ¬£ X t ‚ąą(ő≥ t ,1‚ąíő≥ t ) ¬§ ‚Č§ő≤őĪ t ‚Č§őĶ/2.
Thus, we have that the setE={i‚ąą[n]|H(W i |W <i )‚ąą(n ‚ąíc ,1‚ąín ‚ąíc )} satisfies|E|‚Č§őĶn/2.
Finally, we argue the ‚ÄúFurther" part. Indeed, we have nH(p)= X i‚ąą[n] H(W i |W <i )‚Č• X i‚ąąS\E H(W i |W <i )‚Č•(|S|‚ąí|E|)(1‚ąín ‚ąíc ), where the first equality follows from the chain rule and the last inequality follows from defini- tions ofSandE. Re-arranging one gets that |S|‚Č§ nH(p) 1‚ąín ‚ąíc +őĶn/2‚Č§nH(p)(1+2n ‚ąíc )+őĶn/2‚Č§nH(p)+őĶn.
It remains to prove Lemmas16.5.3and16.5.5which we prove in the rest of this chapter.
328 
16.5.2  Local Polarization To understand howX j compares withX j‚ąí1 , we start with some basic observations about these variables, or more importantly the variablesZ (j) i andZ (j+1) i (recall ( 16.11)). Letiandi ‚Ä≤ bejth level siblings, so that (Z (j) i ,Z (j) i ‚Ä≤ )=P 2 (Z (j‚ąí1) i ,Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ ). Our goal is to compare the pairs of con- ditional entropies (H(Z (j) i |Z (j) <i ),H(Z (j) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j) <i ‚Ä≤ )) with (H(Z (j‚ąí1) i |Z (j‚ąí1) <i ),H(Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) <i ‚Ä≤ )). The col- lection of variables involved and conditioning seem messy, so letus look at the structure ofP n more carefully to simplify the above. We do this by noticing theZ (j) i is really independent of mostZ (j) i ‚Ä≤ (at least for small values ofj) and in particular the set of variables thatZ (j‚ąí1) i and Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ depend on are disjoint. Furthermore these two variables, and the sets of variables that they depend on are identically distributed. Next, we present thedetails.
We begin with a useful notation. Giveni‚ąą[n=2 t ] and 0‚Č§j‚Č§t, let S i,j def = n k‚ąą[n]|i‚Č°kmod 2 t‚ąíj o
.
Note that the‚Ąď 1 ,‚Ąď 2 ‚ąąS i,j need not be siblings at thejth level.
Proposition 16.5.6.For every1‚Č§j‚Č§t and jth level siblings i and i ‚Ä≤ with i<i ‚Ä≤ the following hold: (1) S i,j =S i ‚Ä≤ ,j =S i,j‚ąí1 ‚ą™S i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1
.
(2) n Z (j) k |k‚ąąS i,j o is independent of n Z (j) k |k6‚ąąS i,j o
.
(3) H(Z (j‚ąí1) i |Z (j‚ąí1) <i )=H őľ Z (j‚ąí1) i |Z (j‚ąí1) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j‚ąí1 ,k<i ¬™ ¬∂ =H őľ Z (j‚ąí1) i |Z (j‚ąí1) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ ¬∂
.
(4) H(Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) <i ‚Ä≤ )=H √É Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) n k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ,k<i ‚Ä≤ o !
=H őľ Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ ¬∂
.
(5) H(Z (j) i |Z (j) <i )=H őľ Z (j) i |Z (j) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ ¬∂ =H őľ Z (j) i |Z (j‚ąí1) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ ¬∂
.
329 
(6) H(Z (j) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j) <i ‚Ä≤ )=H őľ Z (j) i ‚Ä≤ | n Z (j) i o ‚ą™Z (j) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ ¬∂ =H őľ Z (j) i ‚Ä≤ | n Z (j) i o ‚ą™Z (j‚ąí1) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ ¬∂
.
Proof.Part (1) follows from the definition ofS i,j and the definition of siblings. Indeed, since i ‚Ä≤ =i+2 t‚ąíj , we havei‚Č°i ‚Ä≤ mod 2 t‚ąíj , which implies the first equality. The second equality follows from the observations thatk 1 ‚Č°k 2 mod 2 t‚ąíj+1 impliesk 1 ‚Č°k 2 mod 2 t‚ąíj (this in turn impliesS i,j‚ąí1 ,S i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ‚äÜS i,j ) and that ifk‚Č°imod 2 t‚ąíj+1 thenk6‚Č°i ‚Ä≤ mod 2 t‚ąíj and vice versa (which in turn implies thatS i,j‚ąí1 andS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 are disjoint. Part (2) follows from the fact that (see Exercise 16.13): Lemma 16.5.7.For every i, the set n Z (j) k |k‚ąąS i,j o is determined completely by ¬© Z k |k‚ąąS i,j ¬™ , and theZ k ‚Äôs are all independent. The first equality in part (3) follows immediately from part (2), and the second uses part (1) and the fact thatZ (j‚ąí1) i is independent of ¬© Z k |k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ,k<i ¬™ (the latter claim follows from the fact thatS i,j‚ąí1 andS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 as disjoint, as argued in the proof of part (1) above). The first equality in part (4) is similar, whereas the second uses the additional fact thatS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 contains no elements betweeniandi ‚Ä≤
. Indeed, the latter observation implies thatH √É Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) n k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ,k<i ‚Ä≤ o !
=H √É Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) n k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ,k‚Č§i o !
. But by part (2),Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ is independent ofZ (j‚ąí1) i and hence we haveH √É Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) n k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ,k<i ‚Ä≤ o !
=H √É Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ |Z (j‚ąí1) n k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ,k<i o !
. The second equality in (4) then follows from parts (1) and (2). The first equalities in parts (5) and (6) are similar to the first equality in part (3) with part (6) using the factthat ¬© k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j |k<i ‚Ä≤ ¬™ = { i } ‚ą™ ¬© k‚ąąS i,j |k<i ¬™
. The second equality follows from the fact that (see Exercise 16.14): Lemma16.5.8.There is a one-to-one map from the variablesZ (j‚ąí1) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ to the variablesZ (j) ¬© k‚ąąS i,j ,k<i ¬™ , and so conditioning on one set is equivalent to conditioning on the other.
To summarize the effect of Proposition16.5.6above, let us name the random variablesU= Z (j‚ąí1) i andV=Z (j‚ąí1) i ‚Ä≤ and further letA= n Z (j‚ąí1) k |k‚ąąS i,j‚ąí1 ,k<i o andB= n Z (j‚ąí1) k |k‚ąąS i ‚Ä≤ ,j‚ąí1 ,k<i ‚Ä≤ o
.
By the proposition above, the conditional entropies of interest (i.e., those ofiandi ‚Ä≤ ) at the (j‚ąí1)th stage areH(U|A) andH(V|B). On the other hand the conditional entropies of interest one stage later (i.e., at thejth stage) areH(U+V|A,B) andH(V|A,B,U). (Here we use that fact thatP 2 (U,V)=(U+V,V).) By part (2) of Proposition 16.5.6we also have that (U,A) and (V,B) are independent of each other. Finally, by examination we also have that (see Exercise 16.15) Lemma 16.5.9.(U,A)and(V,B)are identically distributed.
330 
Thus, our concern turns to understanding the local polarization of two independent and identically distributed bits. If one could ignore the conditioning then this is just a problem about two bits (U,V) and their polarization when transformed to (U+V,V).
In the following lemma, we show how in the absence of conditioning these variables show local polarization effects. (For our application it will be useful for us to allow the variables to be not identically distributed, though still independent.) SupposeH(U)=H(p 1 ) andH(V)= H(p 2 ), then notice thatH(U+V)=H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ) where p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 def =p 1 (1‚ąíp 2 )+p 2 (1‚ąíp 1 ).
In the following lemma we show howH(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ) relates toH(p 1 ) andH(p 2 ).
Lemma 16.5.10.Let p 1 ,p 2 ‚ąą[0,1/2]with p 1 <p 2 andŌĄ‚ąą(0,1/2). Then we have: (1) H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚Č•H(p 2 ).
(2) There existsőł=őł(ŌĄ)>0such that if H(p 1 ),H(p 2 )‚ąą(ŌĄ,1‚ąíŌĄ)then H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚ąíH(p 2 )‚Č•őł.
(3) If H(p 1 ),H(p 2 )‚Č§ŌĄthen H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚Č•(1‚ąí9/log(1/ŌĄ))(H(p 1 )+H(p 2 )).
In particular, for every c<‚ąě, ifŌĄ‚Č§2 ‚ąí9c then H(p 1 )+H(p 2 )‚ąíH(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚Č§(H(p 1 )+H(p 2 ))/c.
(4) If H(p 1 ),H(p 2 )‚Č•1‚ąíŌĄandŌĄ‚Č§1‚ąíH(1/4)then H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚Č•1‚ąí20ŌĄ(1‚ąíH(p 2 )).
In particular, for every c ‚Ä≤ <‚ąě, ifŌĄ<1/(20c ‚Ä≤ )then 1‚ąíH(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚Č§(1‚ąíH(p 2 ))/c ‚Ä≤
.
We defer the proof of the above lemma to Section 16.6.2.
The lemma above essentially proves thatH(U+V) satisfies the requirements for local polar- ization relative toH(U) andH(V), but we still need to deal with the conditioning with respect toAandB. We do this below using some careful applications of Markov‚Äôs inequality.
Lemma 16.5.11.If(U,A)and(V,B)are identical and independent random variables with U,V being elements ofF 2 with H(U|A)=H(V|B)=H(p), then the following hold: (1) For everyŌĄ>0there existsőł>0such that if H(p)‚ąą(ŌĄ,1‚ąíŌĄ)then H(U+V|A,B)‚Č•H(p)+őł.
331 
(2) For every c<‚ąěthere existsŌĄ>0such that if H(p)‚Č§ŌĄthen H(U+V|A,B)‚Č•(2‚ąí1/c)H(p), and if H(p)‚Č•1‚ąíŌĄthen H(U+V|A,B)‚Č•1‚ąí1/c(1‚ąíH(p)).
Proof.Letp a =Pr[U=1|A=a] so thatH(p)=H(U|A)=E A [H(p A )]. Similarly letq b =Pr[V= 1|B=b]. In what follows we consider what happens whenAandBare chosen at random. If H(p A ) andH(q B ) are close to their expectations, then the required polarization comes from Lemma 16.5.10. But ifH(p A ) orH(q B ) can deviate significantly from their expectation, then polarization happens simply due to the fact that one of them is much larger than the other and H(U+V|A=a,B=b)‚Č•max{H(p a ),H(q b )}. The details are worked out below.
We start with part (1). Letőł(¬∑) be the function from part (2) of Lemma 16.5.10so that if H(p 1 ),H(p 2 )‚ąą(ŌĀ,1‚ąíŌĀ) thenH(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚ąíH(p 2 )‚Č•őł(ŌĀ). GivenŌĄ>0 letőł 1 =őł(ŌĄ/2). We prove this part forőł=min n őł 1 9 , ŌĄ 2 36 o >0.

(Note that since (U,A) and (V,B) are identically and independently distributed if one replaces p A byq B in the above equalities, then the equalities still remain valid. We will be implicitly using this for the rest of the proof.) Sincer 1 +r 2 +r 3 =1, at least one of them must be greater than or equal to 1/3. .

.
Thus, with probability at leastŌĄ/18 we haveAsuch thatH(p A )‚Č§(1‚ąíŌĄ) andBsuch thatH(q B )‚Č• 1‚ąíŌĄ/2. Leta,bbe such thatH(p a )‚Č§1‚ąíŌĄandH(q b )‚Č•1‚ąíŌĄ/2. We have (from part (1) of Lemma 16.5.10) H(U+V|A=a,B=a)‚Č•H(q b )‚Č•H(p a )+ ŌĄ 2
.
We conclude that in this case H(U+V|A,B)‚Č•E A [H(p A )]+ ŌĄ 2 36 =H(p)+ ŌĄ 2 36
.
The caser 1 ‚Č•1/3 is similar and also yields H(U+V|A,B)‚Č•H(p)+ŌĄ 2 /36.
Thus, in all cases we have H(U+V|A,B)‚Č•H(p)+őł, which completes the proof of part (1).
We now turn to part (2). We only prove the case whereH(p)‚Č§ŌĄ. The case whereH(p)‚Č•1‚ąíŌĄ is similar and we omit that part (see Exercise 16.16). .

We are now ready to prove the local polarization lemma.
Proof of Lemma 16.5.3.Recall thatX j =E I‚ąľ[n] [Z (j) I ]. LetX j =H(p). Note that conditioned on the value ofX j , for any (j+1)-level siblingsi<i ‚Ä≤ ,Iis equally likely to equaliori ‚Ä≤

Furthermore ifI=ithenX j+1 =H(U+V|A,B) (this follows from ( 16.11) and parts (1) and (5) from Lemma 16.5.6) and ifI=i ‚Ä≤ thenX j+1 =H(V|A,B,U) (this follows from (16.11), parts (1) and (6) from Lemma 16.5.6and the fact thatV|UandV|U+Vhave the same distribution).
With this setup, we are now ready to prove that the sequenceX 0 ,X 1 ,..., satisfy the conditions of local polarization, and furthermore are simple.
334 
We argue the conditions hold for each conditioningI‚ąą{i,i ‚Ä≤ } and so hold without the con- ditioning (the latter holds because the pairs {i,i ‚Ä≤ } make a disjoint cover of [n] and hence for a randomI‚ąľ[n] is equally likely to fall in one of these pairs). The conditionE[X j+1 |X j =a]=a follows from the fact that there is a bijection from (U,V) to (U+V,V), and so H(U+V|A,B)+H(V|A,B,U)=H(U|A)+H(V|B).
Indeed, note that 2ais the RHS and 2X j+1 is the LHS of the above equality.
Now note that (see Exercise 16.17): Lemma 16.5.12.(U,A)and(V,B)are independently and identically distributed.
The variance in the middle condition follows from Lemma16.5.11part (1) and the suction at the ends condition follows from Lemma 16.5.11part (2). Finally simplicity follows from the fact that with probability 1/2,X j =H(U+V|A,B) and with probability 1/2,X j =H(V|A,B,U).
16.5.3  Local vs. Global Polarization Finally we prove Lemma 16.5.5which shows that simple local polarization implies strong polar- ization. We prove this part in two phases. First, we show that in the firstt/2 steps, the sequence shows moderate polarization ‚ÄĒ namely, with all but exponentially small probabilityX t/2 is an inverse exponential int, but with a small constant base (soX t 6‚ąą ¬° őĪ t 1 ,1‚ąíőĪ t 1 ¬Ę for someőĪ 1 <1, but őĪ 1 is close to 1). Next we show that conditioned on this moderate polarization, the sequence gets highly polarized (soX t 6‚ąą ¬° ő≥ t ,1‚ąíő≥ t ¬Ę for anyő≥>0 of our choice), again with an exponentially small failure probability. We start with part (1).

.

We start with the first phase. We consider a potential function ŌÜ j def =min n q X j , q 1‚ąíX j o
.

We now turn to phase two of the polarization. Here we use the fact that ifX t/2 is much smaller thanŌĄ, thenX j is very unlikely to become larger thanŌĄfor anyt/2‚Č§j‚Č§t. Lemma 16.5.15.For allőĽ>0, if X 0 ‚Č§őĽ, then the probability there exists j>0such that X j ‚Č•ŌĄis at mostőĽ/ŌĄ. Similarly if X 0 ‚Č•1‚ąíőĽ, then the probability there exists j>0such that X j ‚Č§1‚ąíŌĄis at mostőĽ/ŌĄ.
The lemma above is a special case of Doob‚Äôs inequality for martingales. We give the (simple) proof below.
Proof.We give the proof for the caseX 0 ‚Č§őĽ. The caseX 0 ‚Č•1‚ąíőĽis symmetrical (see Exer- cise 16.19). Notice that we wish to show that for every integerT>0 Pr ¬∑ max 0‚Č§t‚Č§T {X t }‚Č•ŌĄ  Őß ‚Č§őĽ/ŌĄ.

But E [ Y T ] =E [ Y T‚ąí1 ] =¬∑¬∑¬∑=E [ Y 0 ] =E [ X 0 ] ‚Č§őĽ and this yields the lemma.

Now, if eventE 1 does not occur, then X t/2 ‚Č§2 t/2 ¬∑c ‚ąíZ X 0 ‚Č§2 t/2 c ‚ąíZ
.
The first inequality follows from the subsequent argument. Usingsimplicity, we have with prob- ability 1/2,X 1 ‚Č§(1/c)X 0 ‚Č§X 0 /4 (because of the suction at the ends condition) and with proba- bility 1/2X 1 ‚Č§2X 0 (this follows the bound in the other case and the unbiasedness of theX i s).

.
Proof of Lemma16.5.5.Recall that we wish to show, givenő≥>0, that there existsőĪ<1 and ő≤<‚ąěsuch that for alltwe have Pr ¬£ X t ‚ąą ¬° ő≥ t ,1‚ąíő≥ t ¬Ę¬§ ‚Č§ő≤¬∑őĪ t

16.6  Entropic Calculations In this section, we present the omitted proofs on various properties of entropy and probability that mostly need some calculations.
16.6.1  Proof of Proposition 16.3.1 We begin with the first part, which is a straightforward calculation expanding the definition.
For anyiin the support ofX, letp i denote Pr X [X=i] and letx=argmax i {p i } be the value maximizing this probability. Letp x =1‚ąíő≥. We wish to show thatő≥‚Č§őĪ. We now perform some crude calculations that lead us to this bound.
Ifő≥‚Č§1/2 we have őĪ‚Č•H(X) 338 
= X i p i log 1 p i ‚Č• X i6=x p i log 1 p i (16.15) ‚Č• X i6=x p i log 1 P j6=x p j (16.16) = √É X i6=x p i !
¬∑log √É 1 P j6=x p j !
=ő≥¬∑log1/ő≥ ‚Č•ő≥,(16.17) as desired. In the above, ( 16.15) follows since all summands are non-negative, (16.16) follows since for everyi6=x,p i ‚Č§ P j6=x p j and ( 16.17) follows sinceő≥‚Č§1/2 and so log1/ő≥‚Č•1.
Now ifő≥>1/2 we have a much simpler case since now we have őĪ‚Č•H(X) = X i p i log 1 p i ‚Č• X i p i log 1 p x (16.18) =log 1 p x (16.19) =log 1 1‚ąíő≥ ‚Č•1.(16.20) (In the above, ( 16.18) follows sincep i ‚Č§p x , (16.19) follows since P i p i =1 and ( 16.20) follows from the assumption thatő≥‚Č•1/2.) Butő≥is always at most 1 so in this case also we haveőĪ‚Č•1‚Č•ő≥ as desired.
We now consider the second part, which follows from the previous part via a simple averag- ing argument. Givenyandi, letp i,y =Pr X [X=i|Y=y] and letx y =argmax i {p i,y } be the value maximizing this probability. Lető≥ y =1‚ąíp x y ,y and note thatő≥=E Y [ő≥ Y ]. LetőĪ y =H(X|Y=y) and note again we haveőĪ=E Y [őĪ Y ]. By the first part, we have for everyy,ő≥ y ‚Č§őĪ y and so it follows that ő≥=E Y [ő≥ Y ]‚Č§E Y [őĪ Y ]=őĪ.
16.6.2  Proof of Lemma 16.5.10 The lemma follows in a relatively straightforward manner with parts (3) and (4) using LemmaB.2.3.
Part (1) is immediate from the monotonicity of the entropy function in the interval [0,1/2] (see Exercise 16.7). For 0‚Č§p 1 ,p 2 ‚Č§1/2 we havep 2 ‚Č§p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ‚Č§1/2 and so (see Exercise16.21) H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚Č•H(p 2 ).(16.21) 339 
Next we turn to part (2). LetH ‚ąí1 (x)=psuch that 0‚Č§p‚Č§1/2 such thatH(p)=x. NoteH ‚ąí1 is well defined and satisfiesH ‚ąí1 (x)>0 ifx>0 andH ‚ąí1 (x)<1/2 ifx<1. Let őĪ=őĪ(ŌĄ)=H ‚ąí1 (ŌĄ)(1‚ąí2H ‚ąí1 (1‚ąíŌĄ)) and ő≤=ő≤(ŌĄ)=2H ‚ąí1 (1‚ąíŌĄ)(1‚ąíH ‚ąí1 (1‚ąíŌĄ)) and ő≥=ő≥(ŌĄ)=log((1‚ąíő≤)/ő≤).
Note thatőĪ>0 andő≤<1/2 and soő≥>0. We prove thatH(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚ąíH(p 2 )‚Č•őĪ¬∑ő≥, and this will yield part (2) forőł=őł(ŌĄ)=őĪ¬∑ő≥>0.
First note that sinceH(p 1 ),H(p 2 )‚ąą(ŌĄ,1‚ąíŌĄ), we havep 1 ,p 2 ‚ąą(H ‚ąí1 (ŌĄ),H ‚ąí1 (1‚ąíŌĄ)). Thus, p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ‚ąíp 2 =p 2 (1‚ąí2p 1 )+p 1 ‚ąíp 2 =p 1 (1‚ąí2p 2 )‚Č•H ‚ąí1 (ŌĄ)(1‚ąí2H ‚ąí1 (1‚ąíŌĄ))=őĪ.
Next we consider min p 2 ‚Č§q‚Č§p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 {H ‚Ä≤ (q)}. Note that by Exercise 16.22H ‚Ä≤ (q)=log((1‚ąíq)/q) and this is minimized whenqis maximum. The maximum value ofq=p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 is in turn maximized by using the maximum values ofp 1 ,p 2 =H ‚ąí1 (1‚ąíŌĄ). Thus, we have that min p 2 ‚Č§q‚Č§p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 {H ‚Ä≤ (q)}‚Č• H ‚Ä≤ (ő≤)=ő≥. By elementary calculus we now conclude that H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )‚ąíh(p 2 )‚Č•(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ‚ąíp 2 )¬∑min p 2 ‚Č§q‚Č§p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 {H ‚Ä≤ (q)}‚Č•őĪ¬∑ő≥=őł.
This concludes the proof of part (2).
Next we move to part (3). For this we first describe some useful bounds onH(p). On the one hand we haveH(p)‚Č•plog1/p. Forp‚Č§1/2 we also have‚ąí(1‚ąíp)log(1‚ąíp)‚Č§(1/ln2)(1‚ąíp)(p+ p 2 )‚Č§(1/ln2)p‚Č§2p. And so we haveH(p)‚Č§p(2+log1/p).
Summarizing, we have forp‚Č§1/2, plog(1/p)‚Č§H(p)‚Č§plog(1/p)+2p.(16.22) We now considerH(p 1 )+H(p 2 )‚ąíH(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ). We have H(p 1 )+H(p 2 )‚ąíH(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ) ‚Č§p 1 (log(1/p 1 )+2)+p 2 (log(1/p 2 )+2)‚ąí(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )log(1/(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ))(16.23) ‚Č§p 1 (log(1/p 1 )+2)+p 2 (log(1/p 2 )+2)‚ąí(p 1 +p 2 ‚ąí2p 1 p 2 )log(1/(2p 2 ))(16.24) =p 1 log(2p 2 /p 1 )+p 2 log(2p 2 /p 2 )+2p 1 p 2 log(1/(2p 2 ))+2(p 1 +p 2 ) ‚Č§p 1 log(p 2 /p 1 )+2p 1 p 2 log(1/(p 2 ))+6p 2 (16.25) ‚Č§2p 1 H(p 2 )+7p 2 (16.26) ‚Č§2p 1 H(p 2 )+7H(p 2 )/log(1/p 2 )(16.27) ‚Č§9H(p 2 )/log(1/ŌĄ).(16.28) In the above, ( 16.23) follows from (16.22), (16.24) follows from the fact thatp 1 ‚ó¶p 2 ‚Č§p 1 +p 2 ‚Č§ 2p 2 , ( 16.25) follows from the fact that 3(p 1 +p 2 )‚Č§6p 2 , (16.26) follows from the fact thatp 1 log(p 2 /p 1 )‚Č§ 340 
p 2 , (16.27) follows from (16.22) and (16.28) follows from the subsequent argument. Indeed, by definition ofŌĄand ( 16.22) we havep 2 log(1/p 2 )‚Č§ŌĄ. Using the fact thatp 2 ‚Č§1/2, this implies thatp 2 ‚Č§ŌĄ, which in turn implies log(1/p 2 )‚Č•log(1/ŌĄ). Similarly, we havep 1 log(1/p 1 )‚Č§ŌĄ, which again withp 1 ‚Č§1/2, we havep 1 ‚Č§ŌĄ‚Č§1/log(1/ŌĄ) (where the second equality uses the fact thatŌĄ<1/2). This concludes the proof of part (3).
Finally we turn to part (4). Here we letH(p i )=1‚ąíy i andp i =1/2‚ąíx i fori‚ąą{1,2}. Since ŌĄ‚Č§1‚ąíH(1/4) andH(p i )‚Č•1‚ąíŌĄ, we havex i ‚Č§1/4. By Lemma B.2.4, we have 1‚ąí5x 2 ‚Č§H(1/2‚ąí x)‚Č§1‚ąíx 2 forx‚Č§1/4. Returning to our setup if 1‚ąíŌĄ‚Č•H(1/4) and 1‚ąíy i =H(p i )‚Č•1‚ąíŌĄ, and p i =1/2‚ąíx i , then 1‚ąíx 2 i ‚Č•1‚ąíy i , so x i ‚Č§ p y i
.(16.29) Furthermore,p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 =1/2‚ąí2x 1 x 2 and H(p 1 ‚ó¶p 2 )=H(1/2‚ąí2x 1 x 2 ) ‚Č•1‚ąí20(x 1 x 2 ) 2 ‚Č•1‚ąí20y 1 y 2 =1‚ąí20(1‚ąíH(p 1 ))(1‚ąíH(p 2 )) ‚Č•1‚ąí20ŌĄ(1‚ąíH(p 2 )), where the second inequality follows from ( 16.29). This yields part (4).
16.7  Summary and additional information In this chapter we showed how a very simple phenomenon leads to a very effective coding and decoding mechanism. Even the idea of reducing error-correction to compression is novel, though perhaps here the novelty is in the realization that this can idea can be put to good use.
The idea of using polarization to create a compression scheme, as well as the exact procedure to create polarization are both radically novel, and remarkably effective.
Our description of this compression mechanism is nearly complete. The one omission is that we do not show which columns of the matrixP n should be used to produce the compressed output ‚ÄĒ we only showed that a small subset exists. The reader should know that this aspect can also be achieved effectively, and this was first shown by Taland Vardy [ 164], and adapted to the case of strong polarization by Guruswami and Xia. Specifically there is a polynomial time algorithm that givenp,őĶandcoutputsn‚Č§poly(1/őĶ),P n ‚ąąF n√ón 2 and a setS‚äÜ[n] such that P n is (őĶ,n ‚ąíc )-polarizing for Bern(p) n with unpredictable columnsS, and|S|‚Č§(H(p)+őĶ)n. The details are not very hard given the work so far, but still out of scope of this chapter.
Our analysis of local polarization differs from the literature inthe absence of the use of ‚ÄúMrs.
Gerber‚Äôs Lemma‚ÄĚ due to Wyner and Ziv, which is a convexity claim that provides a convenient way to deal with conditional entropies (essentially implying thatthe conditioning can be ig- nored). In particular, it yields the following statement whose proof can be found as Lemma 2.2 in [ 36].
341 
Lemma 16.7.1.If(U,A)and(V,B)are independent and U,V are binary valued random vari- ables with H(U|A)=H(p)and H(V|B)=H(q), then H(U+V|A,B)‚Č•H(p(1‚ąíq)+q(1‚ąíp)).
The proof of the lemma uses the convexity of the functionH(a‚ó¶H ‚ąí1 (x)) which turns out to have a short, but delicate and technical proof which led us to omitit here. This lemma would be a much cleaner bridge between the unconditioned polarizationstatement (Lemma 16.5.10) and its conditional variant (Lemma 16.5.11). Unfortunately Lemma16.7.1is known to be true only in the binary case whereas our proof method is applicable to larger alphabets (as shown by Guruswami and Velingker [ 87]).

16.9  Bibliographic Notes Polar codes were invented in the remarkable paper by ArńĪkan [ 7] where he showed that they achieve capacity in the limit of large block lengthsn‚Üí‚ąěwithO(nlogn) encoding time and O(nlogn) decoding complexity via the successive cancellation decoder. In particular, ArńĪkan proved that the transformP ‚äót 2 is polarizing in the limit oft‚Üí‚ąě, in the sense that for any fixed ő≥>0, the fraction of indices for whichH(W i |W <i )‚ąą(ő≥,1‚ąíő≥), whereW=P ‚äót 2 Z, is vanishing for larget. In fact, ArńĪkan showed that one could takeő≥=ő≥(t)=2 ‚ąí5t/4 , which led to an upper bound ofn¬∑ő≥=O(1/n 1/4 ) (block) decoding error probability for the successive cancellation decoder.
Soon afterwards, ArńĪkan and Teletar proved that one can takeő≥<2 ‚ąí‚Ą¶(2 ő≤t ) for anyő≤<1/2, which led to improved decoding error probability of 2 ‚ąín ‚ąíő≤ as a function of the block lengthn=2 t
. The fall-off of the parameterő≥innwas referred to as the ‚Äúrate‚ÄĚ of (limiting) polarization.
These works considered the basic 2√ó2 transformP 2 and binary codes. More general trans- forms, and non-binary codes, were considered later in [ 147,109,128]. These results showed that limiting polarization is universal, as long as some minimal conditions are met by the basic matrix being tensored.
The 2012 survey by  ŐßSa Őßso ŐÜglu is an excellent and highly recommended resource for some of the early works on polarization and polar codes [ 36]. Polar codes were widely described as the first constructive capacity achieving codes. Further, polarization was also found to be a versatile technique to asymptotically resolve several other fundamental problems in information theory such as lossless and lossy source coding problem, coding for broadcast, multiple access, and wiretap channels, etc.
However, none of these works yield effective finite length bounds on the block lengthn needed to achieve rates withinőĶof capacity, i.e., a rate at least 1‚ąíh(p)‚ąíőĶfor the binary sym- metric channel with crossover probabilityp. Without this it was not clear in what theoretical sense polar codes are better than say Forney‚Äôs construction, which can also get within any de- siredőĶ>0 of capacity, but have complexity growing exponentially in 1/őĶ 2 due to the need for inner codes of length 1/őĶ 2 that are decoded by brute-force.
343 
A finite length analysis of polar codes, andstrongpolarization where the probability of not polarizing falls off exponentially int, and thus is polynomially small in the block lengthn= 2 t , was established in independent works by Guruswami and Xia [ 88] and Hassani, Alishahi, and Urbanke [ 92]. The latter tracked channel ‚ÄúBhattacharyya parameters‚ÄĚ whereas the former tracked conditional entropies (as in the present chapter) which are a bit cleaner to deal with as they form a martingale. This form of fast polarization made polar codes the first, and so far only known, family with block length and complexity scaling polynomially in 1/őĶwhereőĶis the gap to capacity, This analyis of strong polarization in the above works applied only to the 2√ó2 transofrm and binary case. The strong polarization of the basic 2√ó2 transform was also established for all prime alphabets in [ 87], leading to the first construction of codes achieving the symmetric capacity of all discrete memoryless channels (for prime alphabets) with polynomial complexity in the gap to capacity. However, these analyses relied on ratherspecific inequalities (which were in particular somewhat painful to establish for the non-binary case) and it was not clear what exactly made them tick.
The recent work of the authors and BŇāasiok and Nakkiran [ 11] gave a modular and concep- tually clear analysis of strong polarization by abstracting the properties needed from each local step to conclude fast global polarization. This made the demands onthe local evolution of the conditional entropies rather minimal and qualitative, and enabled showing strong polarization and polynomially fast convergence to capacity for theentireclass of polar codes, not just the binary 2√ó2 case. We followed this approach in this chapter, and in particular borrowed the concepts of variance in the middle and suction at the ends for local polarization from this work.
However, we restrict attention to the basic 2√ó2 transform, and the binary symmetric channel, and gave elementary self-contained proofs of the necessary entropic inequalities needed to es- tablish the properties required of the local polarization step.
Another difference in our presentation is that we described the successive cancellation de- coder for the polarizing transformP ‚äót 2 , which leads to clean recursive description based on a more general primitive of decoding copies of independent but not necessary identical random variables. In contrast, in many works, including ArńĪkan‚Äôs original paper [ 7], the decoding is de- scribed for the transform followed by the bit reversal permutation. The polarization property of the bit reversed transform is, however, notationally simpler to establish. Nevertheless, the transformP ‚äót 2 commutes with the bit reversal permutation, so both the transforms, with or without bit reversal, end up havingidenticalpolarization properties.
344 
Chapter 17 Efficiently Achieving List Decoding Capacity In the previous chapters, we have seen these results related to list decoding: ‚ÄĘ Reed-Solomon codes of rateR>0 can be list-decoded in polynomial time from 1‚ąí p R errors (Theorem 12.2.12). This is the best algorithmic list decoding result we have seen so far.
‚ÄĘ There exist codes of rateR>0 that are ¬° 1‚ąíR‚ąíőĶ,O ¬° 1 őĶ ¬Ę¬Ę -list decodable forq‚Č•2 ‚Ą¶( 1 őĶ ) (and in particular forq=poly(n)) (Theorem 7.4.1and Proposition3.3.4). This of course is the best possible combinatorial result.
Note that there is a gap between the algorithmic result and the best possible combinatorial result. This leads to the following natural question: Question 17.0.1.Are there explicit codes of rate R>0that can be list-decoded in polynomial time from1‚ąíR‚ąíőĶfraction of errors for q‚Č§pol y(n)?
In this chapter, we will answer Question 17.0.1in the affirmative.
17.1  Folded Reed-Solomon Codes We will now introduce a new family of codes called the Folded Reed-Solomon codes. These codes are constructed by combining everymconsecutive symbols of a regular Reed-Solomon code into one symbol from a larger alphabet. Note that we have already seen such a fold- ing trick when we instantiated the outer code in the concatenated code that allowed us to efficiently achieve the BSC p capacity (Section 15.4.1). For a Reed-Solomon code that maps F k q ‚ÜíF n q , the corresponding Folded Reed-Solomon code will mapF k q ‚ÜíF n/m q m
. We will ana- lyze Folded Reed-Solomon codes that are derived from Reed-Solomoncodes with evaluation {1,ő≥,ő≥ 2 ,ő≥ 3 ,...,ő≥ n‚ąí1 }, whereő≥is the generator ofF ‚ąó q andn‚Č§q‚ąí1. Note that in the Reed-Solomon code, a message is encoded as in Figure 17.1.

Form=2, the conversion from Reed-Solomon to Folded Reed-Solomon can bevisualized as in Figure 17.2(where we assumenis even).

More formally, here is the definition of Folded Reed-Solomon codes: Definition17.1.1(Folded Reed-Solomon Code).The m-folded version of an[n,k] q Reed-Solomon code C (with evaluation points{1,ő≥,...,ő≥ n‚ąí1 }), call it C ‚Ä≤ , is a code of block length N=n/m over F q m , where n‚Č§q‚ąí1. The encoding of a message f(X), a polynomial overF q of degree at most k‚ąí1, has as its j‚Äôth symbol, for0‚Č§j<n/m, the m-tuple ¬° f ¬° ő≥ jm ¬Ę ,f ¬° ő≥ jm+1 ¬Ę ,¬∑¬∑¬∑,f ¬° ő≥ jm+m‚ąí1 ¬Ę¬Ę
. In other words, the codewords of C ‚Ä≤ are in one-one correspondence with those of the Reed-Solomon code C and are obtained by bundling together consecutive m-tupleof symbols in codewords of C.
346 
17.1.1  The Intuition Behind Folded Reed-Solomon Codes We first make the simple observation that the folding trick above cannotdecreasethe list de- codability of the code. (We have already seen this argument earlier in Section 15.4.1.) Claim 17.1.2.If the Reed-Solomon code can be list-decoded fromŌĀfraction of errors, then the corresponding Folded Reed-Solomon code with folding parameter m can also be list-decoded fromŌĀfraction of errors.
Proof.The idea is simple: If the Reed-Solomon code can be list decoded fromŌĀfraction of errors (by say an algorithmA), the Folded Reed-Solomon code can be list decoded by ‚Äúunfold- ing" the received word and then runningAon the unfolded received word and returning the resulting set of messages. Algorithm 17.1.1has a more precise statement.
Algorithm 17.1.1Decoding Folded Reed-Solomon Codes by Unfolding INPUT:y=((y 1,1 ,...,y 1,m ),...,(y n/m,1 ,...,y n/m,m ))‚ąąF n/m q m OUTPUT: A list of messages inF k q 1:y ‚Ä≤ ‚Üź(y 1,1 ,...,y 1,m ,...,y n/m,1 ,...,y n/m,m )‚ąąF n q
.
2:RETURNA(y ‚Ä≤ ) The reason why Algorithm17.1.1works is simple. Letm‚ąąF k q be a message. Let RS(m) and FRS(m) be the corresponding Reed-Solomon and Folded Reed-Solomon codewords. Now for everyi‚ąą[n/m], if FRS(m) i 6=(y i,1 ,...,y i,n/m ) then in the worst-case for everyj‚ąą[n/m], RS(m) (i‚ąí1)n/m+j 6=y i,j : i.e. one symbol disagreement overF q m can lead to at mostmdisagree- ments overF q
. See Figure 17.4for an illustration.
f(1)f(ő≥ 2 ) ¬∑¬∑¬∑ f(ő≥ n‚ąí2 ) f(ő≥)f(ő≥ 3 )f(ő≥ n‚ąí1 ) ‚áď f(1)f(ő≥)f(ő≥ 2 )f(ő≥ 3 ) ¬∑¬∑¬∑ f(ő≥ n‚ąí2 )f(ő≥ n‚ąí1 ) Figure 17.4: Error pattern after unfolding. A pink cell means an error: for the Reed-Solomon code it is for RS(m) withy ‚Ä≤ and for Folded Reed-Solomon code it is for FRS(m) withy.
This implies that for anym‚ąąF k q if‚ąÜ(y,FRS(m))‚Č§ŌĀ¬∑ n m , then‚ąÜ(y ‚Ä≤ ,RS(m))‚Č§m¬∑ŌĀ¬∑ n m =ŌĀ¬∑n, which by the properties of algorithmAimplies that Step 2 will outputm, as desired.
The intuition for astrictimprovement by using Folded Reed-Solomon codes is that if the fraction of errors due to folding increases beyond what it canlist-decode from, that error pat- tern does not need to be handled and can be ignored. For example, suppose a Reed-Solomon 347 
f(1)f(ő≥)f(ő≥ 2 )f(ő≥ 3 ) ¬∑¬∑¬∑ f(ő≥ n‚ąí2 )f(ő≥ n‚ąí1 ) ‚áď f(1)f(ő≥ 2 ) ¬∑¬∑¬∑ f(ő≥ n‚ąí2 ) f(ő≥)f(ő≥ 3 )f(ő≥ n‚ąí1 ) Figure 17.5: An error pattern after folding. The pink cells denotesthe location of errors.
code that can be list-decoded from up to 1 2 fraction of errors is folded into a Folded Reed- Solomon code withm=2. Now consider the error pattern in Figure 17.5.
The error pattern for Reed-Solomon code has 1 2 fraction of errors, so any list decoding al- gorithm must be able to list-decode from this error pattern. However, for the Folded Reed- Solomon code the error pattern has 1 fraction of errors which is too high for the code to list- decode from. Thus, this ‚Äúfolded" error pattern case can be discarded from the ones that a list decoding algorithm for Folded Reed-Solomon code needs to consider. This is of course one example‚Äď however, it turns out that this folding operation actuallyrules outa lotof error pat- terns that a list decoding algorithm for Folded Reed-Solomon codeneeds to handle (even be- yond the current best 1‚ąí p Rbound for Reed-Solomon codes). Put another way, an algorithm for Folded Reed-Solomon codes has to solve the list decoding problemfor the Reed-Solomon codes where the error patterns are ‚Äúbunched" together (technically they‚Äôre calledburstyerrors).
Of course, converting this intuition into a theorem takes more work and is the subject of this chapter.
Wait a second...The above argument has a potential hole‚Äď what if we take the argument to the extreme and "cheat" by settingm=nwhereanyerror pattern for the Reed-Solomon code will result in an error pattern with 100% errors for the Folded Reed-Solomon code: thus, we will only need to solve the problem of error detection for Reed-Solomon codes (which we can easily solve for any linear code and in particular for Reed-Solomon codes)? It is a valid concern but we will ‚Äúclose the loophole" by only using a constantmas the folding parameter. This will still keepqto be polynomially large inn(recall that the new alphabet size isq m and let‚Äôs assume thatqis polynomial inn, which is a common assumption) and thus, we would still be on track to answer Question 17.0.1. Further, if we insist on a smaller list size (e.g. one independentofn), then we can use code concatenation to achieve capacity achieving results for codes over smaller alphabets. (See Section 17.4for more.) GeneralCodes.We would like to point out that the folding argument used above is not specific to Reed-Solomon codes. In particular, the argument for the reduction in the number of error patterns holds for any code. In fact, one can prove that for general random codes, with high probability, folding does strictly improve the list decoding capabilities of the original code. (The proof is left as an exercise.) 348 
17.2  List Decoding Folded Reed-Solomon Codes: I We begin with an algorithm for list decoding Folded Reed-Solomon codes that works with agree- mentt‚ąľmRN. Note that this is a factormlarger than theRNagreement we ultimately want.
In the next section, we will see how to knock off the factor ofm.
Before we state the algorithm, we formally (re)state the problem we want to solve (where we wanttto be as small as possible): ‚ÄĘInput: An agreement parameter 0‚Č§t‚Č§Nand the received word: y= Ô£ę Ô£¨ Ô£≠ y 0 y m ¬∑¬∑¬∑ y n‚ąím

y m(i+1)‚ąí1 Ô£∂ Ô£∑ Ô£ł (17.1) The algorithm that we will study is a generalization of the Welch-Berlekamp algorithm (Al- gorithm 12.1.1). However, unlike the previous list decoding algorithms for Reed-Solomon codes (Algorithms 12.2.1,12.2.2and12.2.3), this new algorithm has more similarities with the Welch- Berlekamp algorithm. In particular, form=1, the new algorithm isexactlythe Welch-Berlekamp algorithm (since thenQ(X,Y)=A 0 (X)+Y A 1 (X) whereA 0 andA 1 correspond to the earlier poly- nomialsN(X) andE(X)). Here are the new ideas in the algorithm for the two-step framework that we have seen in the previous chapter: ‚ÄĘStep 1: We interpolate using (m+1)-variate polynomial,Q(X,Y 1 ,...,Y m ), where degree of each variableY i is exactly one. In particular, form=1, this interpolation polynomial is exactly the one used in the Welch-Berlekamp algorithm.
‚ÄĘStep 2: As we have done so far, in this step, we output all "roots" ofQ. Two remarks are in order. First, unlike Algorithms 12.2.1,12.2.2and12.2.3, the rootsf(X) are no longer simpler linear factorsY‚ąíf(X), so one cannot use a factorization algorithm to factorize Q(X,Y 1 ,...,Y m ). Second, the new insight in this algorithm, is to show that all the roots form an (affine) subspace, 1 which we can use to compute the roots.
Algorithm 17.2.1has the details.
1 An affine subspace ofF k q is a set {v+u|u‚ąąS}, whereS‚äÜF k q is a linear subspace andv‚ąąF k q
.

6:RETURNL Correctness of Algorithm17.2.1.In this section, we will only concentrate on the correctness of the algorithm and analyze its error correction capabilities.We will defer the analysis of the algorithm (and in particular, proving a bound on the number of polynomials that are output by Step 6) till the next section.
We first begin with the claim that there always exists a non-zerochoice forQin Step 1 using the same arguments that we have used to prove the correctness of Algorithms 12.2.2and12.2.3: Claim 17.2.1.If ( m+1 )( D+1 ) +k‚ąí1>N, then there exists a non-zero Q ( X,Y 1 ,...Y m ) that satis- fies the required properties of Step1.
Proof.As in the proof of correctness of Algorithms 12.2.1,12.2.2and12.2.3, we will think of the constraints in ( 17.3) as linear equations. The variables are the coefficients ofA i (X) for 0‚Č§i‚Č§ m. With the stipulated degree constraints on theA i (X)‚Äôs, note that the number of variables 350 
participating in (17.3) is D+k+m(D+1)= ( m+1 )( D+1 ) +k‚ąí1.
In the above, we haveD+kvariables forA 0 (X) (since it has degree at mostD+k‚ąí1) and each of thempolynomialsA j (X) forj‚ąą[m] hasD+1 variables each (since each of them have degree at mostD). The number of equations isN. Thus, the condition in the claim implies that we have strictly more variables than equations and thus, there exists anon-zeroQwith the required properties.
Next, we argue that the root finding step works (again using anargument very similar to those that we have seen for Algorithms 12.2.1,12.2.2and12.2.3): Claim 17.2.2.If t>D+k‚ąí1, then every polynomial f ( X ) ‚ąąF q [X]of degree at most k‚ąí1that agrees with the received word in at least t positions is returned by Step6.
Proof.Define the univariate polynomial R ( X ) =Q ¬° X,f ( X ) ,f ¬° ő≥X ¬Ę ,.....f ¬° ő≥ m‚ąí1 X ¬Ę¬Ę
.
Note that due to the degree constraints on theA i (X)‚Äôs andf(X), we have deg ( R ) ‚Č§D+k‚ąí1, since deg(f(ő≥ i X))=deg(f(X)). On the other hand, for every 0‚Č§i<Nwhere ( 17.1) is satisfied we have R ¬≥ ő≥ mi  ŐĀ =Q ¬≥ ő≥ mi ,y mi ,...,y m(i+1)‚ąí1  ŐĀ =0, where the first equality follows from ( 17.1), while the second equality follows from (17.3). Thus, R(X) has at leasttroots. Thus, the condition in the claim implies thatR(X) has more roots than its degree and thus, by the degree mantra (Proposition 5.1.5)R(X)‚Č°0, as desired.
Note that Claims17.2.1and17.2.2prove the correctness of the algorithm. Next, we ana- lyze the fraction of errors the algorithm can correct. Note that the condition in Claim 17.2.1is satisfied if we pick D= ¬Ļ N‚ąík+1 m+1 ¬ļ
.
This in turn implies that the condition in Claim 17.2.2is satisfied if t> N‚ąík+1 m+1 +k‚ąí1= N+m(k‚ąí1) m+1
.
Thus, the above would be satisfied if t‚Č• N m+1 + mk m+1 =N őľ 1 m+1 +mR ¬≥ m m+1  ŐĀ ¬∂ , where the equality follows from the fact thatk=mRN.
Note that whenm=1, the above bound exactly recovers the bound for the Welch-Berlekamp algorithm (Theorem 12.1.6). Thus, we have shown that 351 
Theorem 17.2.3.Algorithm17.2.1can list decode Folded Reed-Solomon code with folding pa- rameter m‚Č•1and rate R up to m m+1 (1‚ąímR)fraction of errors.
