23.8  Bibliographic Notes Theorem 23.1.4is due to Berlekamp, McEliece and van Tilborg [20]. This work seems be the first to apply the lens of computational complexity, and in particularNP-hardness, to coding theoretic problems. Theorem 23.1.3showing the hardness of MaxCut is due to Garey, Johnson and Stockmeyer [ 58]. For more on classicalNP-completeness results the reader is pointed to the text by Garey and Johnson [ 59]. Theorem23.2.2on the hardness of decoding after preprocessing is due to Bruck and Naor [ 24]. The hardness of approximating MaxCut, Theorem23.3.2, is based on the PCP theorem due to Arora et al. [ 9,8]. The hardness of approximating the distance of the nearest codeword, Theorem 23.3.4, is due to Stern [159]. The hardness of decoding up to the minimum distance, Theorem 23.4.2, is due to Dumer, Micciancio and Sudan [41]. We note that the literature includes many significant variations of this result. In particular Guruswami 451 
and Vardy [86] (see also Gandikotta, Ghazi, Grigorescu [57]) have shown that it isNP-hard to decode Generalized Reed-Solomon codes (recall Exercise 5.12). Since these are MDS codes, any decoding hardness is automatically a hardness for a ‚ÄėDistance Bounded Decoding‚Äô problem.
TheNP-hardness of computing the minimum distance of a linear code is dueto Vardy [ 171].
We stress that this isNP-hardness with a deterministic reduction unlike the results proven in this chapter. TheNP-hardness, under randomized reductions, of approximating the minimum distance, Theorem 23.5.3, is from Dumer et al. [41].
452 
Chapter 24 Giving Computational Complexity a helping hand In this chapter we cover a few settings where coding theory turns out to be a useful tool in com- putational complexity, the field that studies the limits of algorithms, and in particular the in- ability of algorithms with limited resources to solve some computational tasks. A central theme in computational complexity is the comparison of resources. For example, if the time taken by an algorithm is viewed as one resource, and a second resource is access to random numbers, then what kind of effect does limiting the second resource haveon the first? For several such questions, tools from coding theory lead to interesting answers (and not always in a predictable way). For instance incommunication complexity, coding theory helps establish that random- ness is a powerful resource. Whereas for other algorithmic tasks, coding theory helps establish that randomness is not so powerful. We will see some of these examples below.
24.1  Communication Complexity Communication complexity studies a very basic task in distributed computing. Some data is partitioned among two entities, say Alice and Bob who wish to compute some function of the combined data, but they wish to minimize the communication between them while doing so.
One simple, but communicationally expensive solution is for one of theplayers to send their entire share of the data to the other player. But such an expensive solution may not always be needed. For instance if the function to be computed is that average of some 2nnumbers where Alice and Bob each havenof the numbers, then Alice only needs to send the sum of their numbers and this may be much smaller number of bits of communication than sending each of the numbers separately. So the communication complexity may depend on the task being performed (aka the function being computed). And as we will see shortly it also depends on whether Alice and Bob have access to randomness, either private or public. Below we model these problems formally and show how error-correcting codes are helpful (and inherently so) for giving an upper bound on the randomized communication complexityof a simple task.
453 
24.1.1  (Deterministic) Communication Complexity In what follows, we letXdenote the space of inputs to Alice andYdenote the space of inputs to Bob. Our examples will useX=Y={0,1} n but we use the more elaborate notion for clarity.
The ‚Äėdata‚Äô is an element (x,y)‚ąąX√óY and we assume Alice knowsxand Bob knowsy. Their task is now modelled by a function f:X√óY‚ÜíO.
Again we will useO={0,1} in this section, but the extra notation will hopefully help us explain the problem.
Communication proceeds in rounds, where in each round a designated player (either Al- ice or Bob) send a bit to the other player. The communicated bit should be a function of the knowledge of the communicating player‚ÄĒnamely their initial input and bits already commu- nicated in previous rounds. After a predetermined number of rounds of communication, one of the players (we will set this to be Bob) outputs the value of the function being computed. How many rounds of communication there are, who speaks in a given round, and what information the communication and output may depend on, are all modeled by aprotocol, formally defined below.
Definition 24.1.1(Deterministic protocol).A (deterministic) protocolő†is given by a tuple (c;P 1 ,...,P c ;ŌÄ 1 ,...,ŌÄ c ;w) where: - c is a non-negative integer denoting the number of bits being communicated.
-{P i ‚ąą{X,Y}|1‚Č§i‚Č§c}determines who speaks in round i. That is, if P i =Xthen Alice speaks and if P i =Y, Bob speaks.
-ŌÄ i :P i √ó{0,1} i‚ąí1 ‚Üí{0,1}describes how the next bit to be communicated depends on the player‚Äôs input and the past bits of communcation.
- w:Y√ó{0,1} c ‚ÜíOis Bob‚Äôs output at the end of the communication.
Next, we define what is means for a protocol to ‚Äėcompute‚Äô a function: Definition 24.1.2(Protocol computation).We say that a (deterministic) protocolő†computes a function f if for every(x,y)‚ąąX√óYwe have f(x,y)=w(y,b 1 ,...,b c ) where for every i we let b i =ŌÄ i (z i ,b 1 ,...,b i‚ąí1 ) where in turn we have z i =x if P i =Xand z i =y otherwise.
454 
In other words, if the player (corresponding to)P i sends the bitb i obtained by applying the functionŌÄ i to their input and the bits exchanged so far, then Bob‚Äôs output equals the value of the functionf.
Finally, we are ready to define the notion ofcommunication complexity: Definition 24.1.3((Deterministic) communication complexity).We define the communication complexity of a protocolő†=(c,...), denotedCC(ő†)to be c. We define the communication com- plexity of a function f , denotedCC(f), to be the minimum, over all protocolső†that compute f , ofCC(ő†).
We make some simple observations about determinitistic protocols inExercise 24.1(WLOG we can assume that Alice speaks last) and Exercise 24.2(ifX= { 0,1 } n , then forany fwe have CC(f)‚Č§n).
24.1.2  Randomized Communication Complexity We now turn torandomizedprotocols. While the literature includes multiple versions of this setting, we only consider one, namely the case of shared randomness. Here we consider a set- ting where Alice and Bob share some elementrchosen uniformly at random from some finite set‚Ą¶, and the bits that they communicate, as well as the output computed by Bob, can addi- tionally depend onr. We say thatő†computesfis for everyx,y, we have that for a random choice ofrthe protocol computesf(x,y) correctly. Formally, a randomized protocol is defined as follows.
Definition 24.1.4(Randomized protocol).A randomized protocolő†is given by a tuple (c;P 1 ,...;ŌÄ 1 ,...;w;‚Ą¶) where: - c:‚Ą¶‚ÜíZ + , such that for each r‚ąą‚Ą¶, c(r)denotes the number of bits being communicated with r being the random bits.
-{P i ‚ąą{X,Y}|1‚Č§i‚Č§c(r)}determines who speaks in round i for randomness choice r‚ąą‚Ą¶.
(Againn, if P i =Xthen Alice speaks and if P i =Y, Bob speaks.) -ŌÄ i :‚Ą¶√óP i √ó{0,1} i‚ąí1 ‚Üí{0,1}describes how the next bit to be communicated depends on the randomness, the player‚Äôs input and the past bits of communcation.
- w:‚Ą¶√óY√ó{0,1} c ‚ÜíOis Bob‚Äôs output at the end of the communication.
We now have the obvious generalization of Definition 24.1.2to randomized protocols: Definition 24.1.5(Randomized protocol computation).We say a randomized protocolő†com- putes a function f if for every x‚ąąX,y‚ąąY, we have Pr r‚ąą‚Ą¶ ¬£ f(x,y)=w ¬° r,y,b 1 ,...,b c(r) ¬Ę¬§ ‚Č• 2 3 , where b i =ŌÄ i (r,z i ,b 1 ,...,b i‚ąí1 )and z i ‚Äôs are as in Definition 24.1.2.
455 
Finally, we can define the notion of randomized communication complexity.
Definition 24.1.6((Randomized) communication complexity).The communication complexity of a randomized protocolő†=(c;...;‚Ą¶), denotedCC R (ő†), is defined as beforemax r‚ąą‚Ą¶ c(r). The randomized communication complexity of a function f , denotedCC R (f), is the minimum, over all randomized protocolső†that compute f , ofCC R (ő†).
We note that the choice of ‚Äėsuccess probability‚Äô as 2 3 in Defintion 24.1.5can be amplified to a larger constant incurring only a constant factor increase inthe communication complexity of the function (see Exercise 24.3).
24.1.3  Separation betweenCC(f)andCC R (f) A very basic question in communication complexity (raised and settled early in the study of this topic) is: Question 24.1.1.What is the largest separation betweenCC(f)andCC R (f), for a function f ?
This was settled by looking at a very simple function: Definition 24.1.7(Equality function).For every n‚Č•1, define EQ n : {0,1} n √ó{0,1} n ‚Üí{0,1} satisfying for anyx,y‚ąą { 0,1 } n : EQ n (x,y)= ( 1ifx=y 0otherwise
.
It is not too hard to show thatCC(EQ n )=nfor every positive integernand we leave this as a (guided) exercise for the reader: see Exercise 24.4.
Theorem 24.1.8.For every positive integer n, we haveCC(EQ n )=n.
Turning to Question24.1.1, or more precisely‚Äď what isCC R (EQ n ), we find the following tight connection between error correcting codes and randomized communication complexity. We restrict our attention to ‚Äėone-way‚Äô communication, defined below, as this already suffices to achieve the optimal randomized communication complexity for the Equality function.
Definition 24.1.9(One-way communication).A protocolő†is aone-wayprotocol if in every round Alice is the speaker (or P i =Xfor every i). We letOW-CC(f)andOW-CC R (f)denote the one-way communication complexities of a function f .
456 
Lemma 24.1.10.Let n‚Č•c‚Č•1be integers. If there is a2 c -ary code with2 n codewords of relative distance 2 3 then OW-CC R (EQ n )‚Č§c.
Conversely, ifOW-CC R (EQ n )‚Č§c then there is a2 c -ary code of relative distance at least 1 3
.
We remark that while many proofs of the forward direction are known that do not seem to involve coding theory, the converse makes it clear that each of these results is in fact a coding- theory result, possibly in disguise.
Proof of Lemma 24.1.10.For the forward direction, letE: {0,1} n ‚Üíő£ N be the encoding func- tion of an error-correcting code with 2 n codewords with|ő£|=2 c
. Furthermore let the relative distance of the code be 2 3 , i.e., for everyx6=y‚ąą{0,1} n we have ‚ąÜ(E(x),E(y))‚Č• 2 3 ¬∑N.
A shared randomness protocol for communication between Alice and Bob can be designed aroundEas follows: 1. Alice and Bob share a random variableiwhereiis distributed uniformly over [N].
2. On inputx‚ąą{0,1} n , Alice sendsE(x) i to Bob.
3. Bob outputs 1 ifE(x) i =E(y) i and 0 otherwise.
It is clear that that protocol above involvescbits of one-way communication from Alice to Bob. It is also clear that ifx=ythen Bob always outputs 1=EQ n (x,y). Now ifx6=y, then with probability at least ‚ąÜ(E(x),E(y)) N over the choice ofiwe haveE(x) i 6=E(y) i
. (Indeed this immedi- ately follows from the definition of‚ąÜ(¬∑,¬∑).) Thus, ifx6=ythen Bob outputs 0=EQ n (x,y) with probability at least ‚ąÜ(E(x),E(y)) N ‚Č• 2 3 , thus establishing that the protocol above outputs the correct answer with probability 2 3 in all cases.
For the reverse direction, lető†be a one-way communication protocol for EQ n
. Let m: {0,1} n √ó‚Ą¶‚Üí{0,1} c be the function that determines Alice‚Äôs message to Bob. Specificallyon inputx‚ąą{0,1} n and randomnessr‚ąą‚Ą¶letm(x,r) denote Alice‚Äôs message to Bob; and further letw(y,r,ŌÉ) denote Bob‚Äôs output on inputy, randomnessrand messageŌÉ‚ąą{0,1} c from Alice. Our error correcting code will be given by the image of the encoding functionE: {0,1} n ‚Üíő£ N , whereő£={0,1} c and N=|‚Ą¶|, with E(x) r =m(x,r) forr‚ąą‚Ą¶(and we associate‚Ą¶with [N]={1,2,...,N}).
To see that ‚ąÜ(E(x),E(y))‚Č• N 3 , 457 
fixx6=yand consider two possible scenarios: the first where Alice getsxand Bob getsy; and a second scenario where both Alice and Bob getyas their input. Ifris such thatE(x) r =E(y) r then Bob outputs the same output in both scenarios. On the other hand we know that in the first scenario Bob outputs 1 with probability at most 1 3 while in the second scenario he outputs one with probability at least 2 3
. Thus the probability that Bob has a different output in the two scenarios is at least 2 3 ‚ąí 1 3 = 1 3
. We conclude that the probability overrthatE(x) r 6=E(y) r is at least 1 3 , or in other words őī(E(x),E(y)) N ‚Č• 1 3 , thus establishing that the image ofEis a code of relative distance at least 1 3
.
1 We note that a easy consequence of our proof above shows that Alice and Bob can solve EQ n by sharingO(logn) bits‚Äď see Exercise 24.5. Next, we note that the proof of the forward direction of Lemma 24.1.10is generally proved using hash functions. As we noted in Proposition20.3.2 (almost universal) hash function and codes are equivalent, which explains why our proof is stated in terms of codes.
24.2  Derandomization and Pseudorandomness One of the richest connections between computational complexityand error-correcting codes occurs in the area ofpseudorandomness. This is a broad area, which includest-wise indepen- dence (see Exercise5.14) andőĶ-bias (see Exercise2.15) as special cases. In this section we see the broader context from which these examples arise, while also seeing the impact of these concepts on the complexity of some simple tasks.
To motivate this broader context, note that randomness is considered a valuable resource for computation: For many tasks, the fastest and simplest algorithmsthat are known are ran- domized. And while these are desirable features, deterministic algorithms have the advantage of repeatability ‚ÄĒ two runs of a deterministic algorithm on the same input will always produce the same output. The broad goal of the field of ‚Äėderandomization‚Äô is to convert randomized al- gorithms into deterministic ones, possibly with a small loss in the running time. (In particular we may wish to preserve polynomial running times.) For us, the term ‚Äėpseudorandomness‚Äô refers to a specific approach to ‚Äėderandomization‚Äô. The central object of attention here is apseudorandom generator, a deterministic (and hopefully ef- ficiently computable) functionG: {0,1} s ‚Üí{0,1} m wheres‚Č™m, which takessbits of random- ness and outputsmpseudorandom bits. The idea of pseudorandomness is to use the output of a pseudorandom generator instead ofmgenuinely random (independent, unbiased) bits as inputs to a randomized algorithm, and hope that the pseudorandom bitsfoolthe algorithm.
We now turn to formalizing the notion offoolingbeing alluded to above. Recall that a ran- domized algorithmArunning on inputxcan be viewed as a deterministic running on two in- puts, the actual inputxand a random stringy. Recall further (see Appendix C) that a random- ized algorithmAis said to compute a functionfcorrectly if Pr y [A(x,y)=f(x)]‚Č•2/3, where 1 If the protocol hasone-sided error, and always outputs 1 whenx=y, then one can conclude that the encoding mapEdefines a code with relative distance at least 2 3
.
458 
A(x,y) denotes the output of aglrothmAon inputxand randomnessy. We are now ready to define pseudorandomly fooling the algorithmA.
Definition 24.2.1.Given an algorithm A, input x and parameterőĶ‚ąą[0,1], we say that a genera- tor GőĶ-fools the pair(A,x)if  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ Pr y‚ąą{0,1} m ¬£ A(x,y)=f(x) ¬§ ‚ąíPr z‚ąą{0,1} s ¬£ A(G(z))=f(x) ¬§  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ ‚Č§őĶ.
In other words the probability with whichAoutputs the correct answer with pseudoran- dom strings isőĶ-close to the probability it outputs the correct answer with pure randomness.
And since the correct answer is reported with probability at least2/3 with pure randomness, ifőĶ<1/6 then even with pseudorandom strings, the correct answer is output with probabil- ity strictly greater than 1/2. And to revert to ‚Äėderandomization‚Äô: note that the probability with whichA(G(z)) computes any vaule, can be computed deterministically by 2 s runs ofA(just runAwith every determinitistic choice ofz‚ąą{0,1} s ). In particular ifAis a polynomial time algorithm ands=O(logm) then runningAa total of 2 O(logm) times only takes polynomial time.
The above discussion thus motivates pseudorandomness and the challenge of finding gen- erators that fool our algorithm of choice. Now the most ambitious studies try to find generators that fool all polynomial time algorithms, but this study is out of scope for this chapter. In this chapter we will focus on generators that fool some very simple algorithms. We will use one al- gorithm as our example, though the approaches to fooling this algorithm will lead us to fairly general pseudorandom generators.
24.2.1  Maxt-SAT and a randomized algorithm The principal problem we use to capture the effect of pseudorandomness is MAXt-SAT, a prob- lem involving finding a good assignment tonBoolean variablesx 1 ,...,x n
. Recall that a Boolean variableis one that can be assigned one of two values 0 (representing false) and 1 (representing true). Aliteralis either a variable or its negation (where the negation of a variablex, denoted ¬¨xis true if and only ifxis false). For positive integert, at-clauseis the disjunction (logical OR) oftdistinct literals, where the disjunction oftliterals‚Ąď 1 ,...,‚Ąď t
. denoted‚Ąď 1 ‚ą®¬∑¬∑¬∑‚ą®‚Ąď t , is true if and only if at least one of the literals‚Ąď i is true. Finally an assignmentx 1 ‚Üźa 1 ,...,x n ‚Üźa n where a 1 ,...,a n ‚ąą{0,1} is said to satisfy a clauseC=‚Ąď 1 ‚ą®¬∑¬∑¬∑‚Ąď t if at least one of the literals is true (i.e., assigned the value 1 under the assignment). Given a collectionő¶=(C 1 ,...,C m ) ofm t-clauses over variablesx 1 ,...,x n , and an assignmenta‚ąą{0,1} n to the variablesx 1 ,...,x n , we let val(ő¶,a) denote the fraction of clauses ofő¶satisfied bya, and we let opt(ő¶)=max a {val(ő¶,a)}.
The goal of the MAXt-SAT problem is to determine, given such a collectionő¶, an assignmenta that maximizes val(ő¶,a).
Problem 24.2.2.(Maxt-SAT) GIVEN:m t-clauses C 1 ,...,C m over n variables x 1 ,...,x n
.
OUTPUT:An assignmenta‚ąą{0,1} n such thatval(ő¶,a)=opt(ő¶).
459 
MAXt-SAT is known to beNP-hard for allt‚Č•2 (see Exercise24.6). So we often look for approximation algorithms, i.e., a polynomial time algorithm that findsan assignment satisfying a large number of clauses though not necessarily the assignment that maximizes the number of satisfied clauses. Formally, Definition 24.2.3(Approximation Algorithm).ForőĪ‚ąą[0,1]an algorithm A that takes as input aMAXt-SATinputő¶and outputs a value A(ő¶)‚ąąRis said to be anőĪ-approximation algorithm if for allő¶we have őĪ¬∑opt(ő¶)‚Č§A(ŌÜ)‚Č§opt(ő¶).
In other words the algorithm outputs a number close to opt(ő¶) and this approximation gets better asőĪ‚Üí1. As we will see next, it is easy to find non-trivialőĪ-approximation algorithms with polynomial running time. The following gives the simplest such algorithm, though it is randomized.
Algorithm 24.2.1RANDOMt-SAT INPUT:t‚ąíSAT formulaő¶onnvariables OUTPUT: A rational number in [0,1] 1:Picka‚ąą { 0,1 } n uniformly at random 2:RETURNval(ő¶,a) In other words, the algorithm simply ignores its input and picks a random assignment and outputs the number of clauses satisfied by this random assignment. And yet this turns out to be a non-trivial approximation algorithm, or at least leads to one. We start to analyze this next.
First note that the number of clauses ofő¶satisfied by a random assignment is a random variable and so the output of RANDOMt-SATis a random variable that can vary between 0 andm. What we see below is that the expectation of this random variable is atleast (1‚ąí2 ‚ąít )¬∑1‚Č•(1‚ąí2 ‚ąít )¬∑opt(ő¶), since clearly opt(ő¶)‚Č§1.
Lemma 24.2.4.For every inputő¶toMAXt-SATwith m t-clauses , we have E [ RANDOMt-SAT(ő¶) ] ‚Č•1‚ąí2 ‚ąít Proof.Letadenote the assignment chosen by RANDOMt-SAT(ő¶) and letZ i denote the indicator variable that is 1 if thei-th clauseC i is satisfied bya. Then we haveE[Z i ]‚Č•1‚ąí2 ‚ąít since the only way thatC i =‚Ąď 1 ‚ą®¬∑¬∑¬∑‚ą®‚Ąď t is not satisfied is if‚Ąď 1 =¬∑¬∑¬∑=‚Ąď t =0, an event that happens with probability most 2 ‚ąít
. Specifically if two of the literals are complementary (i.e., are of theformx j and¬¨x j ) then the clause is always satisfied, else the literals use distinct variables and in which case the probability that‚Ąď 1 =¬∑¬∑¬∑=‚Ąď t =0 is exactly 2 ‚ąít
. (Note that we are crucially using the fact that at-clause hastdistinct literals.) We now note that val(ő¶,a)= 1 m m X i=1 Z i 460 
and use the linearity of expectations to conclude that E [ val(ő¶,a) ] = 1 m m X i=1 E [ Z i ] ‚Č•1‚ąí2 ‚ąít , as desired.
Unfortunately the bound above is only on the expected value of theoutput of the algorithm and so does not immediately lead to a 1‚ąí2 ‚ąít -approximation algorithm. It turns out that we can replace the expectation bound with a high probability bound with aslight loss in approximation factor‚Äď more precisely there exists a randomized (1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ‚ąíőĶ)-approximation algorithm that runs in timeO(m/őĶ) for anyőĶ>0 (see Exercise 24.7). This leads to the following natural question: Question 24.2.1.Does there exists a determinitic polynomial time (ideally close to linear runtime) ¬° 1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ¬Ę -approximation algorithm for theMAXt-SATproblem?
Note that in the above we want to get rid of the randomness as wellas the loss ofőĶin the ap- proximation ratio. We answer this question in the next couple of sections.
24.2.2  Limited independence To convert our algorithm into a deterministic one, we use the concept of limited independence.
We already saw this concept in Exercise2.14but now we define this concept as a special case of pseudo-randomness.
Given a generatorG: {0,1} s ‚Üí{0,1} n and setT‚äÜ[n] letG| T (¬∑) denote the projection ofG(¬∑) to the coordinates ofT.
Definition 24.2.5.We say that a generator G: {0,1} s ‚Üí{0,1} n is a t-wise independent generator if it satisfies the following condition: For every subset T‚äÜ[n]with|T|=t, we have G| T (z)is uniformly distributed over{0,1} t if z is distributed uniformly over{0,1} s
.
Exercises 2.14and5.15imply the following result: Lemma 24.2.6.1. LetC be a linear[n,k,d] 2 code with encoding function E:F k 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 with the property that its dual C ‚ä• is a code of distance at least t+1. Then E is a t-wise independent generator.
2. For infinitely many n and t there is a linear t-wise independent generator G: {0,1} s ‚Üí {0,1} n with seed length s‚Č§ ¬° t 2 ¬Ę logn.
461 
One can show that the bound on the seed length in the second itemabove is essentially tight (even without the condition of linear generator)‚Äď see Exercise 24.8.
We now turn to applying this notion of pseudorandomness to ‚Äėfooling‚Äô algorithms. Specifi- cally we applyt-wise independent generators to our algorithm RANDOMt-SAT. Unfortunately, for this application, we will resort to a weaker definition of fooling than the one above. Roughly, our definition (Definition 24.2.7) expects that the distribution of outputs of a randomized al- gorithm is nearly preserved when uniform randomness is replaced by the output of a pseudo- random generator. The following definition weakens this requirement and only says that the expected output is nearly preserved. As we will see even this weak definition turns out to be quite useful.
Definition 24.2.7.Given an algorithm A whose output is in the range[0,1], inputxand param- eterőĶ‚ąą[0,1], we say that a generator GőĶ-weakly foolsthe pair(A,x)if  ŐĄ  ŐĄ E y‚ąą { 0,1 } m ¬£ A ¬° x,y ¬Ę¬§ ‚ąíE z‚ąą { 0,1 } s [ A ( x,G(z) )]  ŐĄ  ŐĄ ‚Č§őĶ.
Our next lemma shows that the RANDOMt-SATis weakly fooled by at-wise independent generator.
Lemma 24.2.8.Let G: {0,1} s ‚Üí{0,1} n be a t-wise independent generator. Then G0-weakly fools RANDOMt-SAT.
Proof.We simply go through the proof of Lemma 24.2.4. We note that for a fixedt-clauseC, the probability that it is satisfied by a uniformly random assignmentais the same as the probability that it is satisfied by the output ofG, since the satisfaction only depends on thetvariables participating inCandGist-wise independent. Thus the expected output of RANDOMt-SAT on inputő¶, whether its randomness is uniform or the output of the generatorG, is simply the probability that a single clause is satisfied by a uniform assignment. It follows that RANDOM t-SATis 0-weakly fooled byG.
Corollary 24.2.9.There is a deterministic1‚ąí2 ‚ąít -approximation algorithm forMAXt-SATwith running time O t ¬≥ n t 2 +1  ŐĀ time on inputs of length n.
Proof.Lető¶be a formula of lengthn, and so it has at mostnvariables andnclauses. Our deterministic algorithm on inputő¶acts as follows: (1) Picks at-wise independent generator G: {0,1} s ‚Üí{0,1} n fors‚Č§t/2‚Ćąlog 2 (n+1)‚ĆČ(as guaranteed by Lemma 24.2.6). (2) Runs RANDOM t-SAT on every random input in image ofG, thus running it on 2 s random assignments. (3) Output the maximum number of clauses satisfied by these 2 s random assignments. The formal algorithm is present in Algorithm 24.2.2.
We now analyze the performance of Algorithm 24.2.2. Note that Step4takes timeO(n) since one can compute the number of clauses satisfied by a fixed assignmentin the same time. Run- ning it on 2 s =n t 2 assignments takes timeO ¬° n 1+t/2 ¬Ę
. Since the expected value of the output of RANDOMt-SAT on random output ofGis (1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ), by Lemma 24.2.8, it follows that the max- imumvat the end is also at least (1‚ąí2 ‚ąít )‚Č•(1‚ąí2 ‚ąít )opt(ő¶) and so this algorithm is a 1‚ąí2 ‚ąít - approximation algorithm.
462 
Algorithm 24.2.2De-randomized RANDOMt-SAT INPUT:t‚ąíSAT formulaő¶onnvariables OUTPUT: A rational number in [0,1] 1:s‚Üź ¬° t 2 ¬Ę logn 2:PickG: {0,1} s ‚Üí{0,1} n to be at-wise independent generator 3:v‚Üź0 4:FORz‚ąą { 0,1 } s DO 5:v‚Üźmax ( v,val ( ő¶,G ( z ))) 6:RETURNv The approximation factor given by the algorithm above turns out to be best possible for t‚Č•3 for a polynomial time algorithm, assumingNP6=P. But the running time is not the best possible and we improve it in the next section.
24.2.3őĶ-bias and almost limited independence We now turn to an alternate notion of pseudorandomness that at first glance seems to be un- related to the notion oft-wise independence, but which we will later show is also closely re- lated. The notion we now introduce is that of fooling ‚Äėlinear‚Äô functions. Recall that a function ‚Ąď:F n 2 ‚ÜíF 2 is linear if there exists coefficientőĪ 1 ,...,őĪ n ‚ąąF 2 such that‚Ąď(y 1 ,...,y n )= P m i=1 őĪ i y i , where all arithmetic is in the fieldF 2
.
Definition 24.2.10.We say that a generator G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 isőĶ-biased if for non-zero every linear function‚Ąď:F n 2 ‚ÜíF 2 we have  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ Pr z‚ąąF s 2 [ ‚Ąď ( G(z) ) =1 ] ‚ąí1/2  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ ‚Č§őĶ.
Note that over the uniform distribution, the probability that‚Ąď(¬∑) takes on the value 1 is ex- actly 1 2 for linear functions‚Ąď(recall Lemma 3.1.13). So the condition above is requiring that‚Ąďis preserving its output distribution to withinőĶwhen its inputs are pseudorandom (i.e., the out- put ofG) as compared to the case when they are uniformly random. In other wordsGőĶ-fools every linear function.
We now recall the notion of ‚Äėbalanced‚Äô codes (mentioned in Exercise 2.15) and show how they lead toőĶ-biased generators quite immediately.
Definition 24.2.11.We say that a linear code C‚äÜF N 2 isőĶ-balanced if for every pair of distinct codewordsx,y‚ąąC we have őľ 1 2 ‚ąíőĶ ¬∂ N‚Č§‚ąÜ(x,y)‚Č§ őľ 1 2 +őĶ ¬∂ N.
We now state the simple equivalence betweenőĶ-biased generators andőĶ-balanced codes (see Exercise 24.9).
463 
Proposition 24.2.12.1. Let A‚ąąF K√óN 2 be the generator matrix of anőĶ-balanced code. Let A i ‚ąą F K 2 denote the ith column of A. Then the function G: [N]‚ÜíF K 2 given by G(i)=A i is an őĶ-biased generator.
2. Let G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 be anőĶ-biased generator. Let A:F n√ó2 s 2 be the matrix whosezth column (for s‚ąą { 0,1 } s is given by A z =G(z). Then A generates anőĶ-balanced code.
As a corollary, using the best known constructions of binary linear error-correcting codes, we get the following: Lemma 24.2.13.For everyőĶ>0and every sufficiently large n, there exists aőĶ-biased generator G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 , where s=log(n/őĶ 2 )+O(1). Furthermore if we allow s=log(n 2 /őĶ 2 )+O(1), or s= log(n/őĶ 3 )+O(1)then this generator runs in timepoly(n/őĶ).
While fooling linear functions may not sound all that exciting, itturns out thatőĶ-bias gives us a lot more. In particular, ifőĶis exponentially small inn, then the output distribution is almost uniform, and even whenőĶis larger, the output ‚Äėalmost‚Äô fools small tests. In turn this gives away to speed up the MAXt-SAT approximation algorithm from the previous section, for a tiny loss in the approximation guarantee.
We start by showing that the output distribution is close to uniform ifőĶis really small. As a quick aside we recall the following definiton: Definition 24.2.14.The variation distance between two distributionsD 1 ,D 2 with the same sup- port set S is given by 1 2 X x‚ąąS  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ Pr X‚ąľD 1 [ X=x ] ‚ąíPr Y‚ąľD 2 [ Y=x ]  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ
.
We present some implications of the total variational distance inExercise 24.10.
Lemma 24.2.15.ForőĶ‚Č•0if G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 is anőĶ-biased generator, then the output of G is2 n ¬∑őĶ close to uniform in total variation distance.
Proof.We prove an even stronger statement. Specifically for everyx‚ąąF n 2 we prove that|Pr z [G(z)= x]‚ąí2 ‚ąín |‚Č§2őĶ. The lemma follows from the fact that the total variation distanceof the distribu- tionG(z) from the uniform distribution overF n 2 equals 1 2 P x |Pr z [G(z)=x]‚ąí2 ‚ąín |‚Č§2 n őĶ.
Fixx‚ąąF n 2
. Let1 x (y) denote the function that is 1 wheny=xand 0 otherwise and note that our goal is to get a close estimate ofE z [1 x (G(z))]. ForőĪ‚ąąF n 2 , letL őĪ (y)= P n i=1 őĪ i y i and let Ōá őĪ (y)=(‚ąí1) L őĪ (y)
. Note thatŌá őĪ is a real-valued function. On the one hand we have, by theőĶ- biased property ofG, that|E x [Ōá őĪ (G(z))]|‚Č§2őĶ, for everyőĪ‚ąąF n 2 ‚ąí{0}. Now note that we can write 1 x (y)= 1 2 n Ô£ę Ô£≠ X őĪ‚ąąF n 2 :Ōá őĪ (x)=1 Ōá őĪ (y)‚ąí X őĪ‚ąąF n 2 :Ōá őĪ (x)=‚ąí1 Ōá őĪ (y) Ô£∂ Ô£ł
.
464 
We can now get our estimate onE z [1 x (G(z))] as follows. We haveE z [Ōá 0 (G(z))]=1 sinceŌá 0 is the constant function. Thus E z [|1 x (G(z))‚ąí2 ‚ąín |]=E z Ô£ģ Ô£į  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ 1 2 n Ô£ę Ô£≠ X őĪ‚ąąF n 2 ‚ąí{0}}:Ōá őĪ (x)=1 Ōá őĪ (G(z))‚ąí X őĪ‚ąąF n 2 :Ōá őĪ (x)=‚ąí1 Ōá őĪ (G(z)) Ô£∂ Ô£ł  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ Ô£Ļ Ô£Ľ ‚Č§ 1 2 n X őĪ‚ąąF n 2 ‚ąí{0}  ŐĄ  ŐĄ Ōá őĪ (G(z))  ŐĄ  ŐĄ ‚Č§ 2 n ‚ąí1 2 n (2őĶ) ‚Č§2őĶ.
We remark that the bound above is not tight. It is known thatőĶ-biased distributions are őĶ¬∑2 n/2 -close to uniform, but proving that requires some additional ingredients from Discrete Fourier Analysis. Exercise 24.13shows how to get this improvement.
We now define the notion of ‚Äėalmost-limited independence‚Äô.
Definition 24.2.16.For positive integer t and realőī‚Č•0we say that a generator G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 is aőī-almostt-wise independent generatorif it satisfies the following condition: For every subset T‚äÜ[n]with|T|=t, we have thatG| T (z)isőī-close (in total variation distance) to being uniformly distributed over{0,1} t when z is distributed uniformly over{0,1} s
.
The following lemma connects small-biased generators to almost-limited-independence.
The key here is to note that the projection of the output of anőĶ-biased generator to any sub- set oftoutput bits retains theőĶ-bias property. The lemma then follows immediately from Lemma 24.2.15.
Lemma 24.2.17.For every positive integer t andőĶ‚Č•0if G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 is anőĶ-biased generator, then G is also aőī-almost t-wise independent generator, forőī=őĶ¬∑2 t
.
Proof.LetGF s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 be anőĶ-biased generator and letT‚äā[n] be a set of cardinality|T|=t. Let G| T :F s 2 ‚ÜíF t 2 be the projection of the output ofGto the coordinates ofT, i.e., G| T (z)=(x i 1 ,...,x i t ) whereG(z)=(x 1 ,...,x n ) andT={i 1 ,...,i t }.
It follows immediately from the definition ofőĶ-bias thatG| T is alsoőĶ-biased. (Gfools all linear functions, and in particular linear functions supported onTalso.) Thus, by applying Lemma 24.2.15toG| T , we get that the output ofG, restricted to the setT, isőĶ¬∑2 t close in total variation distance to the uniform distribution on {0,1} t
.
Since this holds for every subsetT‚äÜ[n] of cardinalityt, it follows thatGisőĶ2 t -almost-t-wise independent.
We now show how this leads to an improved algorithms for MAX-t-SAT.
The following lemma is quite similar to Lemma 24.2.8and we defer the proof to Exercise24.11.
465 
Lemma 24.2.18.Let G: {0,1} s ‚Üí{0,1} n be aőī-almost t-wise independent generator. Then G ( 2őī ) -weakly foolsRANDOMt-SAT.
Using the lemma above we easily get the following application to MAXt-SAT.
Corollary 24.2.19.There is a deterministic1‚ąí2 ‚ąít -approximation algorithm forMAXt-SATwith running time O(4 t ¬∑n 5 )time on inputs of length n.
Proof.We prove this by setting parameters appropriately. LetŌÜbe at-CNF formula of lengthn.
In particular note thatŌÜhas at mostnvariables andm‚Č§nclauses. We set őī= 1 2 t+2 m and let őĶ=2 ‚ąít őī.
LetG:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 be aőĶ-biased distribution, and hence (by Lemma 24.2.17)Gisőī-almostt-wise independent. It follows from Lemma 24.2.18thatG2őī-weakly fools RANDOMt-SAT. In other words E Z [val(ő¶,G(z))]‚Č•1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ‚ąí2őī.
In particular, there exists azsuch that val(ő¶,G(z))‚Č•1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ‚ąí2őī=1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ‚ąí2 ‚ąí(t+1) /m.
But val(ŌÜ,x) is an integral multiple of 1/mand so if val(ő¶,G(z))‚Č•1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ‚ąí2 ‚ąít+1 /m, then val(ő¶,G(z))‚Č•1‚ąí2 ‚ąít
.
Thus, we only need to search over a space of size 2 s to find such azand by Lemma 24.2.13we can takes=log(n 2 /őĶ 2 )+O(1) so that 2 s =O(n 2 /őĶ 2 )=O(4 t n 4 ). Computing val(ŌÜ,x) for any given xtakesO(n) time, leading to the claimed running time.
The result above is a significant improvement on the previous boundin Lemma24.2.9, but we are not done yet. It turns out one can combine the notions ofőĶ-bias andt-wise indepen- dence to get an even better construction of almost-t-wise independent distributions leading us to our final result.
Lemma 24.2.20.Let G 1 :F s 2 ‚ÜíF ‚Ąď 2 be anőĶ-biased generator. Let G 2 :F ‚Ąď 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 be alineart-wise independent generator. Then G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 given by G(z)=G 2 (G 1 (z))is an(őĶ¬∑2 t )-almost t-wise independent generator.
466 
Proof.Fix a setT‚äÜ[n] with|T|=tand letG| T andG 2 | T denote the projection ofGandG 2 (resp.) to the coordinates inT, so thatG| T (z)=G 2 | T (G 1 (z)). We claim thatG| T isőĶ-biased and so by Lemma 24.2.17its output isőĶ2 t close to the uniform distribution. Since this holds for everyT‚äÜ[n] with|T|=t, we get thatGis an (őĶ¬∑2 t )-almostt-wise independent generator.
We now prove the claim above. LetL:F t 2 ‚ÜíF 2 be a non-zero linear function. SinceG 2 :F ‚Ąď 2 ‚Üí F n 2 is also linear, we have thatG 2 | T is also a linear function and so L ‚Ä≤ =L‚ó¶G 2 | T :F ‚Ąď 2 ‚ÜíF 2 is also a linear function. We claim thatL ‚Ä≤ is not the zero-function. Once we show this we are done, since by theőĶ-bias ofG 1 we have:  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ Pr z‚ąąF s 2 [ L(G| T (z))=1 ] ‚ąí1/2  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ =  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ Pr z‚ąąF s 2 ¬£ L ‚Ä≤ (G 1 (z))=1 ¬§ ‚ąí1/2  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ  ŐĄ ‚Č§őĶ.
We thus turn to showingL ‚Ä≤ is non-zero. SinceLis non-zero, there must be ana‚ąąF t 2 such that L(a)6=0. Furthermore sinceG 2 ist-wise independent, we must haveG 2 | T (y) is uniformly dis- tributed for a uniformly randomy‚ąąF ‚Ąď 2 and in particular, there must exists ab‚ąąF ‚Ąď 2 such that G 2 | T (b)=a. But then we have L ‚Ä≤ (b)=L(G 2 | T (b))=L(a)6=0.
This concludes the proof.
One natural question to as is whether theGas constructed above is also anőĶ-biased source: see Exercise 24.14.
Lemma 24.2.21.For everyőī>0, integer t and every sufficiently large n, there exists a polynomial time computableőī-almost t-wise independent generator G:F s 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 , where s=2(t+log1/őī+loglogn)+O(1).
Proof.We let ‚Ąď= t 2 ¬∑logn, and letG 2 :F ‚Ąď 2 ‚ÜíF n 2 be the map given by Lemma 24.2.6, soG 2 ist-wise independent and linear.
We next set őĶ=2 ‚ąít őī and s=log(‚Ąď 2 /őĶ 2 )=2t+log(‚Ąď 2 /őī 2 )+O(1)=2t+log(1/őī 2 )+2loglogn and letG 1 :F s 2 ‚ÜíF ‚Ąď 2 be anőĶ-biased map given by Lemma 24.2.13. LettingG=G 2 ‚ó¶G 1 and apply- ing Lemma 24.2.20yields our lemma.
As with Corollaries24.2.9and24.2.19, we get the following corollary with the above con- struction (see Exercise 24.15).
Corollary 24.2.22.For everyőī>0, there is a deterministic1‚ąí2 ‚ąít ‚ąíőī-approximation algorithm forMAXt-SATwith running time O(4 t /őī 2 ¬∑n(logn) 2 )time on inputs of length n.
467 
24.3  Hardcore Predicates We now turn to some applications of coding theory to the foundations of cryptography. In this section we consider the task of constructing a ‚Äėhardcore predicate‚Äô for a ‚Äėone-way permutation‚Äô.
We start by defining these two notions. We start with the second notion which is a very basic and ubiquitous notion in cryptography.
Recall that {0,1} ‚ąó =‚ą™ n‚ąąZ ‚Č•0 {0,1} n
.
Definition 24.3.1.We say that f: {0,1} ‚ąó ‚Üí{0,1} ‚ąó is apermutationif (1) f is length-preserving, i.e., for every n andx‚ąą{0,1} n we have f(x)‚ąą{0,1} n and (2) f is invertible, i.e., there exists a function f ‚ąí1 : {0,1} ‚ąó ‚Üí{0,1} ‚ąó such that for everyx‚ąą{0,1} ‚ąó we have f ‚ąí1 (f(x))=x.
The essence of cryptography are functions that are ‚Äėvery hard‚Äôto compute. To this end, we define the following notions of a function being hard: Definition 24.3.2.(1) A function f: {0,1} ‚ąó ‚Üí{0,1} ‚ąó to beeasyif there exists a polynomial time algorithm A that computes f , i.e., for everyx‚ąą{0,1} ‚ąó , A(x)=f(x).
(2) A function f is said to be (merely)hardif it is not easy.
‚ÄėVery hard‚Äô-ness is a quantified strengthening of the last notion above: (3) For a functionőĪ:Z ‚Č•0 ‚Üí[0,1], we say that f isőĪ-hard if for every polynomial time algo- rithm A and for every sufficiently large n‚ąąZ ‚Č•0 we have Pr x‚ąą{0,1} n ¬£ A(x)=f(x) ¬§ ‚Č§őĪ(n).
(4) Finally we say that f isvery hardif for every constant c‚Č•0we have f is 1 n c )-hard.
We are finally ready to define one-way permutations: Definition 24.3.3.A permutation f: {0,1} ‚ąó ‚Üí{0,1} ‚ąó is said to be aone-way permutationif (1) f is easy and (2) f ‚ąí1 is very hard.
In what follows we will switch from functions on arbitrarily long inputs to functions on finite length inputs by fixing an input length, denotedk, forf(orb). When we talk about ‚Äėpolynomial time‚Äô computability, it will be good to keep in mind thatfis defined on all inputs and the algo- rithm must run in polynomial time for all input lengths.
While a one-way permutation is in itself a very powerful object, ifa one-way function comes augmented with a hardcore bit, or ‚Äėhardcore predicate‚Äô, it becomes even more useful. We ex- plain this notion next.
Definition 24.3.4.ForőĶ>0, we say that a Boolean function (or predicate) b: {0,1} k ‚Üí{0,1}is said to be anőĶ-hardcore predicate for a permutation f: {0,1} k ‚Üí{0,1} k if 468 
(1) b is easy and (2) b‚ó¶f ‚ąí1 is(1/2+őĶ)-hard.
We say that b is a hardcore predicate for f is b is k ‚ąíc -hardcore for f for every c‚Č•0.
In other wordsb(x) is easy to compute givenx, but very hard to compute givenf(x). Note that ifbis a hardcore predicate forf, thenf ‚ąí1 must be very hard (see Exercise 24.16).
More importantlybcaptures the essence off ‚ąí1 being very hard, in that it gives a ‚Äėsingle bit‚Äô of information aboutx, namelyb(x), that is very hard to guess (better than random) givenf(x).
Exercise 24.17illustrates the utility of one-way permutations in building very general pseudo- random generators.
If the functionfis crafted carefully, then some very natural predicates turn outto be hard- core. (For the reader familiar with the RSA function we mention that there a hardcore predicate isb(x)=x 1 , the most significant bit of the input.) However for such simple predicatesbit is also possible to construct one way functionsffor whichbis not a hardcore predicate. (See Exercise 24.18.) In what follows we will consider one-way functions with some ‚Äėpadding‚Äô.
Definition 24.3.5.We say that a function F: {0,1} k+‚Ąď ‚Üí{0,1} k+‚Ąď is an‚Ąď-padding (or simply padding) of f: {0,1} k ‚Üí{0,1} k , if for everyx‚ąą{0,1} k andi‚ąą{0,1} ‚Ąď we have F(x,i)=(f(x),i).
Exercise 24.18shows how paddings of one-way permutations pose obstacles to some natu- ral candidates for hardcore predicates.
In view of this difficulty of constructing ‚Äėgeneric hardcore predicates‚Äô, (i.e., ones that work for every one-way function) the next theorem will likely come as apleasant surprise: it says that for every one-way function, there is a padded version that admits a simple hardcore predicate.
The key ingredient in our construction is an efficiently list-decodable binary code. We recall the notion below.
The following is a simple (algorithmic) generalization of Definition 7.2.1: Definition 24.3.6.A function E: {0,1} k ‚Üí{0,1} n is(ŌĀ,L)-efficiently-list decodable if there is an efficient algorithm L such that (1) For everyy‚ąą{0,1} n , D(y)‚äÜ{0,1} k satisfies|D(y)|‚Č§L and (2) For everyx‚ąą{0,1} k such that‚ąÜ(E(x),y)‚Č§ŌĀn, we havex‚ąąD(y).
In what follows we will show that a random bit of the encoding ofx, under a nicely list de- codable codeE, is a hardcore predicate for every padded one-way function.
Lemma 24.3.7.Let f: {0,1} k ‚Üí{0,1} k be a one-way permutation and let E: {0,1} k ‚Üí{0,1} n be a (1/2‚ąíőĶ,L)-efficiently list decodable code with n=2 t for some integer t. Let F: {0,1} k+t ‚Üí{0,1} k+t be the padding of f given by F(x,i)=(f(x),i)for i‚ąą{0,1} t
. Finally let b: {0,1} k+t ‚Üí{0,1}be given by b(x,i)=E(x) i where i‚ąą{0,1} t is viewed as an element of[n]and E(x) i denotes the ith coordinate of E(x). Then b is(2őĶ)-hardcore for F.
469 
Proof.The proof is follows mostly from definitions. Assume for contradiction thatbis not 2őĶ- hardcore forFand letA: {0,1} k+t ‚Üí{0,1} be a polynomial time algorithm showing this. Note that this implies Pr x,i ¬£ A ¬° f(x),i ¬Ę =E(x) i ¬§ ‚Č• 1 2 +2őĶ.
We show how to useAto invertfwith high probability.
Our algorithm for invertingfproceeds as follows: Algorithm 24.3.1INVERTf INPUT:w=f(x)‚ąą { 0,1 } k for unknownx OUTPUT:x‚ąą { 0,1 } or ERROR 1:Computey= ¬° y 1 ,...,y n ¬Ę ‚ąą{0,1} n given byy i =A(w,i).
2:S‚ÜźD(y).‚ä≤Run the list decoderDforE 3:FOReveryx ‚Ä≤ ‚ąąSDO 4:IFf(x ‚Ä≤ )=yTHEN 5:RETURNx ‚Ä≤ 6:RETURNERROR The running time of Algorithm24.3.1is clearly bounded by a polynomial inn.
We now analyze the probability that it successfully outputsxgivenw=f(x) as input. Let G= ¬Ĺ x|Pr i ¬£ A ¬° f(x),i ¬Ę =E(x) i ¬§ ‚Č• 1 2 +őĶ ¬ĺ
.
We note that 1 2 +2őĶ‚Č§Pr x,i ¬£ A ¬° f(x),i ¬Ę =E(x) i ¬§ ‚Č§Pr x [x‚ąąG]+ 1 2 +őĶ,(24.1) where the upper bound follows from the following argument. Note that Pr x,i ¬£ A ¬° f(x),i ¬Ę =E(x) i ¬§ =Pr x [x‚ąąG]¬∑Pr x,i ¬£ A ¬° f(x),i ¬Ę =E(x) i |x‚ąąG ¬§ +Pr x [x6‚ąąG]¬∑Pr x,i ¬£ A ¬° f(x),i ¬Ę =E(x) i |x6‚ąąG ¬§ ‚Č§Pr x [x‚ąąG]+Pr x,i ¬£ A ¬° f(x),i ¬Ę =E(x) i |x6‚ąąG ¬§ , which from definition ofGproves the upper bound in ( 24.1). Further note that from (24.1), we can conclude that: Pr x [ x‚ąąG ] ‚Č•őĶ.
We now argue that ifx‚ąąGthen our inversion algorithm above outputsx. To see this note that by definition ofGwe have that ‚ąÜ ¬° E(x),y ¬Ę ‚Č§ őľ 1 2 ‚ąíőĶ ¬∂ n.
Now by the property of our list-decoder we have thatx‚ąąS,D(y). In turn this implies that our algorithm will reportxas output (because of line 4), as desired.
470 
To apply the lemma above to get a theorem showing the existence of a hardcore predicate (one that works for everyőĶ(k)=k ‚ąíc ) for every padded one-way permutation one needs a few additional steps. Specifically one would need to know more about thehardness off, and use this to choose the functionőĶ(k) which in turn will determinen(k) and in turn this determines the running time of the inverter off. We omit these details in this book (see Section 24.6for some pointers).
24.4  Average case complexity Next we turn to another application of error-correcting codes incomplexity theory ‚ÄĒ this time to relate the average case complexity of problems to the worst-case complexity of related prob- lems. Most results in computational complexity refer only to the worst-case complexity. For instance if we assumeP6=NPand we know that some problemő†isNP-hard this implies that for every polynomial time algorithmAthere exists an instancexfor whichA(x)6=ő†(x). But such x‚Äôs may be extremely rare. Average-case complexity seeks much stronger notions of hardness one that may imply that for every algorithmAand sufficiently largen, Pr[A(x)6=ő†(x)]‚Č§ 1 10
.
(I.e., at least 10% of the inputs are hard, when the inputs are chosen uniformly at random.) While in general such problems that are hard on uniformly chosen random inputs seems quite hard, coding theory seems to lead to such hardness quite easily (though for problem which are much harder thanNP-complete ones). In this section we describe such a hardness result for the ‚Äėpermanent‚Äô problem, which relies on the local decodability of Reed-Muller codes. Later we abstract this reduction into a generic one based on locally decodable codes (see Chapter??.
Definition 24.4.1.For a fieldFand matrix M‚ąąF n√ón with M= ¬£ m i,j ¬§ i,j‚ąą[n] the Permanent of M, denotedperm(M)is the quantityperm(M)= P ŌÄ‚ąąS n Q n i=1 m i,ŌÄ(i) where S n ={ŌÄ: [n]‚Üí[n] : ŌÄinvertible}is the set of permutations over[n].
Note that the permanent ofMis very similar to the notion of the determinant of a ma- trix. Specifically, the determinant can be defined as P ŌÄ‚ąąS n sign(ŌÄ) Q n i=1 m i,ŌÄ(i) where sign :S n ‚Üí {‚ąí1,+1} is an easy to compute (though somewhat long to define) function.Indeed the perma- nent is arguably simpler in that it is simpler to define than the determinant. Remarkably while the more ‚Äėcomplex‚Äô determinant is easy to compute, the permanent turnsout to be quite hard.
The following theorem hints at its complexity.
Theorem 24.4.2.The permanent isNP-hard. Specifically there are polynomial time algorithms A and B such that for every instanceŌÜof satisfiability, we have A(ŌÜ)=(F q ,n,M)where M‚ąąF n√ón q and B(perm(M))=1if and only ifŌÜis satisfiable.
We remark that the theorem above merely bounds the complexity of thepermanent from below. It is not known to be reducible to satisfiability (and indeed not believed to be inNP).
471 
Turning to the theme of this section, the result above indicates that the permanent is hard in the usual ‚Äėworst-case‚Äô sense. But as we will see below this problem is as hard on random instances as on worst case For this section we use the following definition of easy on the average. (This definition is similar in spirit to the definition of ‚Äėhard on average‚Äô in Definitions 24.3.2.) Definition 24.4.3.Given a function f and family of distributions D={D n } n where D n is a dis- tribution on inputs of length n, we say that the pair(f,D)is inaverage polynomial timeif there exists a polynomial time algorithm A such that for every n, we have Pr x‚ąľD n ¬£ f(x)6=A(x) ¬§ ‚Č§ 1 10
.
Remark24.4.4.The threshold of being incorrect with probability at most 1 10 is an arbitrary choice.
Unfortunately, unlike the case of randomized polynomial time, thenotion of average polynomial time is not robust with respect to this threshold. So it is conceivable (likely?) that some pairs(f,D) would be in average polynomial time under the above definition, but not so if the threshold were set to something smaller such as 1 20
.
Theorem 24.4.5.Let D={D n } n be a family of distributions given as follows: Given n, let p= p(n)‚ąą[30n,60n]be a fixed prime. Let D n be a uniformly random matrix M‚ąąF n√ón p
. Then if the pair(perm,D)is in average polynomial time, thenperm(M)is computable in randomized polynomial time for every matrix in the support of D.
Proof.By assumption on (perm,D) being in average polynomial time, letAbe a polynomial time algorithm such that Pr M‚ąąF n√ón p ¬£ A(M)6=perm(M) ¬§ ‚Č§ 1 10
.(24.2) Define the polynomial over the ‚Äėmatrix‚Äô of variablesX= ¬© X i,j ¬™ i,j‚ąą[n] : P(X)= X ŌÄ‚ąąS n n Y i=1 X i,ŌÄ(i) , whereS n is as in Definition 24.4.1. Note thatP(X) is a multivariate polynomial of degreeninn 2 variables. Further for anyM‚ąąF n√ón p , we have perm(M)=P(M).
The above implies that the vector c= ( P(M) ) M‚ąąF n√ón p is a valid codeword in RM(p,n 2 ,n) (recall Definition 9.1.3). Further define y= ( A(M) ) M‚ąąF n√ón p
.
472 
Then by (24.2), we have őī(c,y)‚Č§ 1 10
.(24.3) It can be verified that the above fraction of errors is less thanhalf the distance of RM(p,n 2 ,n) and hence for example we could use Theorem 13.3.8. However, the issue with this is that the block length of RM(p,n 2 ,n) isp n 2 and so a runtime that is polynomial inp n 2 (which is what we will get from Theorem 13.3.8) is too expensive for our purposes.
However, the point to note is that we do not need to decodeyin its entirety. I.e. we are not interested in computing the entire codewordcbut given anyM‚ąąF n√ón p , we are interested in computingc M only. In other words, we are interested inlocallydecodingyfor which we can use results from Section??. Specifically, the algorithm is to return the output of the followingcall: RM-LOCAL-CORRECTOR y ¬° M;p,n,n 2 ¬Ę
.
There is one ‚Äėcatch‚Äô in that the setting of locally correctable codes we assume oracle access toy, which we do not have directly. However, note that sinceAis polynomial time algorithm, we can computeA(M) for any givenM‚ąąF n√ón p in polynomial time (so as long as the locally correcatable code that runs in poly(n) time (which means we will need to callAat most those many times), our overall algorithm will run in poly(n) time as well.
Specifically, Proposition??implies that we need to make at mostp‚ąí1 calls toAand the run- time is poly(p,n 2 ). Thus, the overall runtime (by our choie ofp) is poly(n), as desired. Finally, for the correctness of our reduction, by Lemma??our reduction works as long as őī(c,y)‚Č§ 1 6 őľ 1‚ąí n+1 p‚ąí1 ¬∂
.
Note that the above and ( 24.3) impliy that we would be done if can ensure 1 6 őľ 1‚ąí n+1 p‚ąí1 ¬∂ > 1 10 , which in turn is satisfied if n+1 p‚ąí1 < 1 15 , which in turn in satisfied if p>15(n+1)+1.
The above is satisfied by our choice ofp(the existence of such apfollows from known results) and the proof is complete.
Next, we generalize the essence of the above proof to show a general worst-case hardness to average case hardness result.
Lemma 24.4.6.Let N=2 n and K=2 k and C: {0,1} K ‚Üí{0,1} N 473 
be a ¬° polylogN, 1 10 ¬Ę -locally decodable code. Given a function f: {0,1} k ‚Üí{0,1} let F: {0,1} n ‚Üí{0,1} be the function F=f‚ó¶C.
If F is in average polynomial time, then f is computable in randomized polynomial time (on worst-case instances).
Proof.This proofs turns out to follow essentially by combining the relevant definitions.
LetAbe a polynomial time algorithm that shows thatFis in average polynomial time. So, Pr x‚ąą{0,1} n [ A(x)6=F(x) ] ‚Č§ 1 10
.
LetT(A)‚ąą{0,1} N denote the vectorT(A) x =A(x). Changing notations, and usingF=f‚ó¶C, we have őī(T(A),C(T(f)))‚Č§ 1 10
.
LetDbe the local decoding algorithm forC. We then have for everyy‚ąą{0,1} k , with probability at least 2 3 (over the coin tosses ofD),D T(A) (y)=f(y).
But note thatD T(A) can be simulated in polylogKtime sinceDruns in polylogN=polylogK time given oracle access toAand oracle access toAamounts to simply computingAwhich takes polynomial time ink. Since logN=O(logK)=O(k) we have thatfcan be computed on every input in time polynomial in the input (by a probabilistic algorithm which is correct with probability at least 2/3 on every input).
While the lemma above is quite general, we warn the reader that the resulting functionF is not necessarily simple to compute even iffis. Note that the natural way to computeFon an input of lengthnwould be to computefon all inputs of lengthk, encode the resulting 2 k - bit long string, and then read the specific bit of the encoding. Assumingk=‚Ą¶(n), this takes time 2 ‚Ą¶(n)
. So in particular this does not necessarily lead to functions inFthat are inNPthat are hard on average (assuming for example thatfis inNPbut not inP). However the reduction can be applied to functionsfof higher complexity, say exponential time computable, to get new functions in the same class that are also hard on average. We omit the details in this section.

Exercise 24.12.In this exercise, we will define and consider properties oforthonormal basis. In addition to being of interest in its own right, it‚Äôll help us improve the bound in Lemma 24.2.15 (see Exercise24.13below).
Let n‚Č•1be an integer and we will consider the vector spaceR 2 n
. We note that for every vector v‚ąąR 2 n can be equivalently be defined by a function f v : { 0,1 } n ‚ÜíRsuch that f v (x)=v x where we identify the positions invby elementsx‚ąą { 0,1 } n
. In this exercise we will switch back and forth between the vector and function representations.
We say a subset B‚äÜR 2 n is an orthonormal basis if ‚ÄĘ | B | =2 n ‚ÄĘFor everyu‚ąąB, we have k u k 2 def = q P 2 n i=1 u 2 i =1 ‚ÄĘFor everyu6=v‚ąąB, we have ‚Ć© u,v ‚Ć™ def = P 2 n i=1 u i ¬∑v i =0.
We first begin with some example orthonormal basis and then provesome properties of such basis.
1. Prove that the set { e x } x‚ąą { 0,1 } n (i.e. the function f e x (y)= ( 1ifx=y 0otherwise ) is an orthonormal basis. (This is the so calledstandard basis.) 2. For any subset S‚äÜ[n], define the function Ōá S (y)= 1 p 2 n ¬∑ ( ‚ąí1 ) P i‚ąąS y i
.
Prove that the set of vectors { v S } S‚äÜ[n] , where for each Sv S is the function corresponding to Ōá S , is an orthonormal basis. (This is the so calledFourier basisand we have seen this basis already in Exercise 24.8.) 3. Prove that the vectors in an orthonormal basis are linearly independent (hence the term basis).
4. Letu‚ąąR 2 n (in other wordsu= ( u 1 ,...,u 2 n ) is its representation in the standard basis). Then the following is true for any orthonormal basis B: u= X v‚ąąB ‚Ć© u,v ‚Ć™ ¬∑v.

Exercise 24.18.We consider the following results related to a padding of a permutation (recall Definition 24.3.5): 1. Prove that if f: { 0,1 } k ‚Üí { 0,1 } k is a one-way permutation, then the k-padding of f is also a one-way permutation.
2. Let b: { 0,1 } 2k ‚Üí { 0,1 } be a predicate that only depends on the second half of its input bits.
Then prove that b is not a hardcore predicate for some one-way permutation.
480 
24.6  Bibliographic Notes Communication complexity is due to Yao [?]. The separation of deterministic and probabilisitic communication complexity of the Equality function is from [?]. Our proof here is by now folk- lore. More details on communication complexity can be found in the text by Kushilevitz and Nisan [?].
The notion of limited independence go back to Carter-Wegman. The connection between limited independence and linear codes is due to Alon, Babai and Itai[?]. The notion ofőĶ-bias and almost limited-independence, and the relationship between the two, are from Naor and Naor [?]. Some of the specific connections between codes andőĶ-biased spaces are from Alon, Goldreich, H√•stad, and Peralta [?]. Vadhan‚Äôs book on Pseudorandomness [?] is an excellent source for much more on this topic.
The notion of hardcore predicates and connections to pseudo-randomness is from the works of Blum and Micali [?] and Yao [?]. The connections to list-decoding of codes, and to coding the- ory in general, is from the seminal work of Goldreich and Levin [?].
The hardness of permanent on the average is from the work of Lipton??building on the work of Beaver and Feigenbaum [ 15]. Quantitative strengthenings of this result leading to our Theorem??and Exercise??are from Gemmell et al. [ 60] and Gemmell and Sudan [62]. The general connection between local decoding and worst-case toaverage-case reductions is from Sudan et al. [ 163].
481 
482
